“I love Tracy Wolfson because I love her pride for her school and I think that's going to be what I'm like,” Barbanente said. “I know in broadcasting, you're not supposed to be like, ‘Oh, I love this,’ but what you're passionate about is what you're passionate about.”

While it wasn’t until high school that Barbanente realized she had a chance to play softball at a Power 4 level, the second baseman had her mind set on pursuing broadcasting at a young age. After a WGN field trip in seventh grade, she knew broadcasting was what she wanted to do.

“It gives perspective that no amount of prep can truly offer,” Jack Rachinsky, who frequently broadcasts alongside Barbanente, said. “I can do all the stats, all the charts in the world, but I haven't been in a locker room, I haven't been in a huddle.”

When Barbanente’s on the mic, her experience as a competitor offers unique insights and brings deeper, more personal connections to the athletes she’s covering. Barbanente understands the emotional highs and lows of competition and how a single play can change a game, or how a player can struggle individually yet still contribute to a team’s success.

“I know how it feels to win and also go 0-for-3 and feel like I didn't do anything, but understand the team is so much bigger than myself,” Barbanente said. “It’s those little moments that are so big in our lives that maybe you don't really see as an outsider looking in.”

That lived experience allows for a better storytelling approach with a deeper sense of awareness. Understanding what athletes go through behind the scenes, while highlighting details that might otherwise be overlooked, gives the audience a more complete picture of the game.

“For any sport, the mentality of student-athletes remains the same,” Rachinsky said, “So you have that depth, or you can add a new layer, because you can go on about a story about how you deal with a certain situation.”

Barbanente is also finding a way to connect through language. In addition to broadcast journalism, Barbanente studies Spanish.

“I do want to work in the MLB one day, if that's the path that I choose to go down, so understanding that you have 30 to 50 percent of your players are Spanish speakers,” Barbanente said. "If you can be able to communicate with them, I think it just opens up so many more jobs.”

When it comes to bringing broadcasting and softball together, Barbanente often finds her skills and techniques from each overlapping. She said the way she prepares to perform in “big moments” for softball is the way she prepares to go on air.

Whether Barbanente’s stepping into the batter’s box or sitting behind a microphone, the routine is the same. The pressure might look different on the surface, but internally, she approaches both environments with the same mindset.

“When I struggle in softball, it's when I don't trust myself and when I struggle in broadcasting is probably when I don't trust myself,” Barbanente said. “It’s like (the) same thing, different font and I don't think it can actually be more clear, because the more I think about it, the more those two really translate.”

Recently, Barbanente saw her two worlds collide. After Penn State defeated Saint Francis on April 21, the junior got the untraditional opportunity to conduct a Big Ten Plus post-game interview with her teammate, Cara Bohner.

The interview became an unexpected bucket-list moment for Barbanente. She had been introduced to the idea of interviewing one of her own teammates, but wasn’t sure the opportunity would present itself as the season was winding down.

“It was so cool to be able to do that, and especially with Cara Bohner, somebody who has grown up in the area watching Penn State softball games,” Barbanente said. “(Bohner is) somebody who doesn't really get the spotlight as much as she should for the impact that she really has on our team and our teammates.”

One of the most noticeable features a broadcaster presents is their personality. Barbanente has shown her impact in both the dugout and broadcast, with her key characteristic coming from her personability.

"She’s always got a smile on her face, she's hilarious, she's very positive, a great person to be around,” Rachinsky said. “That shines through, especially when she's gotten more comfortable, where you can see that personality shine and you want to tune in for longer.”

The biggest embrace that Barbanente has taken away from both softball and broadcasting is knowing it’s possible to succeed in multiple pursuits. She approaches her goals with determination, and said success depends on effort and mindset.

“If you want it, go get it — everything's there for you, it's a matter of how you're gonna attack it,” Barbanente said. “In no place am I the softball player I want to be today, or the broadcaster I want to be today, but I understand the ceilings out there and just to keep climbing.”

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: PHOTO ESSAY | Addison Court tenants left to ponder an uncertain future amid sale, redevelopment talks
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
At 82, John Harris is many things.

A cancer survivor currently in remission. A member of and frequent donor to various organizations such as Amnesty International, Earthjustice and the American Civil Liberties Union. A longtime Democrat who briefly attended the first and third No Kings protests.

Harris is also a 1966 Penn State graduate who enjoys speaking with current and former students during his visits to the Corner Room, where his order always includes peanut butter on toast and a cup of coffee.

Harris said he enjoys the close proximity of the Corner Room to his home of 28 years, Arnold Addison Court — also known as Addison Court, since both are located in downtown State College.

Addison Court, located at 120 E. Beaver Ave. between the Pugh Centre, Schlow Centre Region Library and a CATA office, is currently managed by the real estate company Calibre Residential. It was opened in 1994 to provide affordable housing to elderly and disabled individuals in the State College community under the low income housing tax credit program.

Addison Court’s 30-year lease within the program expired on Dec. 31, 2024. The building is currently in talks to be redeveloped following its sale by its current owner, Beaver Court Associates.

“I've been able to continue to go there, living here very conveniently, by foot and that's been very nice because I don't have a car and don't drive,” Harris said.

Should Addison Court be torn down, Harris said he’s still unsure where he’ll go next.

Lily Persio, who talks with Harris when she works in the Corner Room, said her conversations with him can range from being about the restaurant’s coffee to his letters to the editors sometimes featured in local newspapers.

“I think it's cool to see someone who's been here all their life and they still love it,” Persio, a graduate student studying business administration, said. “It's nice to see people that love this place so much that they've always wanted to stay. I feel like sometimes I just always wanna stay.”

Harris said the sale and redevelopment of Addison Court would cause there to be less affordable housing in State College for lower and middle class people as well as the disabled.

“To have it torn down, all of their ability to live at the center of town will be destroyed in favor of student housing," Harris said. "That will be five people to a bedroom and huge profits to the real estate industry, which is unfortunate."

Real estate development firm PrimeCore Properties presented a preliminary land development plan to the borough in early January. The plan outlines that a mixed-use structure, called Encore, will be built in place of Addison Court, featuring residential, office and commercial spaces.

“If you look around now, you see all these buildings getting knocked down for these luxury apartments and, unfortunately, Addison Court might become one,” Emily Morgan, a CVS employee who first met Harris around 2021, said. “I just see what State College might be going for — that look of they don't want to have these ‘townies,’ maybe, 'cause they think, ‘Well, this is a college town,’ and they don't want the elderly and those with disabilities to coincide with the town.”

During a State College Borough Council meeting on Jan. 12, several Addison Court tenants, some of whom were accompanied by family members, made public comments expressing concerns that the building's residents were being forced out of the downtown area to make room for more student housing in the borough.

In a public comment during the council’s meeting on April 20, Addison Court resident Kathryn Santoro said she’s unable to find an apartment in State College able to support her disability nor where she can use the Housing Choice Voucher Program, also called Section 8, to make housing more affordable.

Santoro said she can’t move out of State College due to limited transportation options that can help her get to the borough.

“We can walk around anywhere without any problems. I can walk at any hour of the day with my dog and not have one issue,” Sandi Tyger, another Addison Court tenant, said. “People don't want to leave.”

Before his resignation, former council member Josh Portney introduced a proposal to place the building under eminent domain, which is described as “the power of the Commonwealth to take private property for public use in return for just compensation.”

However, Addison Court doesn’t meet the requirements set by the 2006 Property Rights Protection Act, which requires the building to be declared condemned in order to be seizeable by the borough.

The borough is considering buying Addison Court, according to comment from Borough Manager Tom Fountaine following the April 20 borough council meeting. But, he said there wasn't enough information to make a decision.

This uncertainty hasn’t stopped Harris from contacting borough and county officials — like Pennsylvania House Rep. Scott Conklin (D-Centre) — and otherwise speaking out about Addison Court in any way he can.

On April 17, tenants received a letter signed by Calibre Residential Vice President James Hook, which said the building was now contracted to a new organization, though it didn’t state which one.

The letter also said a group, later revealed during the April 20 borough council meeting to be Continental Real Estate Management, would assist in relocating tenants out of Addison Court.

On April 3, PrimeCore requested an amendment to the height restrictions around Addison Court to make Encore better match the Pugh Centre in appearance, leading to an April 20 presentation by PrimeCore CEO Ara Kervandjian and former State College Zoning Officer Herman Slaybaugh.

The council voted 6-1 to have the request be reviewed by the Planning Commission to see how the borough can best assist Addison Court’s tenants.

Several Addison Court residents were also present on April 20, many of whom having attended the council’s Jan. 12 meeting.

In a public comment, Rodger Weir, who’s lived in Addison Court since 1999, said after he expressed concerns about lapses in the building’s maintenance, management started to slip lease violations under his door about his service animal. He said these violations “looked like retaliation.”

“I think the attitude in the county was to say, ‘The landlord put up the money to put up the building and he has the right to do anything he wants to with it, including selling it,’” Harris said. “I paid rent in the building for the whole 28 years, starting at $500 then and $1,100 now, and that constitutes a kind of investment in the building on my part and I think all the tenants have the same status and we all think that it’s nice to have the diversity downtown that we’ve brought. There isn’t any need for student housing in exactly this spot.”

MORE PHOTO ESSAYS

title: Despite Penn State’s party culture, peer support helps students in recovery redefine their identities
author: Mia Debelevich | News Editor
description:
Editor’s Note: Two of the names in this story have been changed to protect the identity of the individuals involved. The Daily Collegian has verified through fact-checking that these individuals exist.

Penn State is regularly boasted as a top party school, ranking No. 12 on Niche’s 2026 list. For most students, that's a selling point. For a student in recovery, it can make State College feel like the worst possible place to get clean.

Every day, the pregames, the darties, the bars and the fraternity houses all crawl to life. Being surrounded by that can make sobriety difficult.

Despite this, many students are achieving it. Penn State’s Collegiate Recovery Community (CRC), founded in 2011 by a group of professors to support students recovering from substance disorders, has become one of the most established student recovery programs in the country, according to Director Jason Whitney.

In 2022, Whitney published a study in Alcoholism Treatment Quarterly (ATQ) examining 12 students enrolled in recovery programs at three universities officially designated as party schools. The study found that building community allowed students an alternative identity and social habit in an environment organized around substance use.

Whitney was a student at the University of Colorado when he decided to get sober. He realized redefining the city of Boulder meant redefining his perception of the university’s culture.

“It felt at first that it was impossible to get sober in a place like Boulder,” Whitney said. “Like, if I was pulling out of alcohol and drug use, that I was pulling out of the main culture thing that was happening.”

Once Whitney gained traction in recovery, he started replacing the “party” in front of “town” with the identities he was growing into.

“It felt like a fitness town,” Whitney said. “It felt like a climbing town, because I started getting into climbing. And it started to feel like a kind of entrepreneurial, techie town.”

Whitney said he rediscovered Boulder as he rediscovered himself. He thinks students at the CRC do the same in State College.

The benefits of peer support show up across broader research, too. A nationwide survey published in the National Library of Medicine found the mean GPA of 486 students at 29 collegiate recovery programs was 3.22 — higher than the overall undergraduate student body at their respective institutions.

Whitney said many might guess recovering students’ future prospects are average at best. However, that’s not where they’re landing. Students in these peer recovery programs academically perform at some of the highest rates he has ever studied, he said.

When many students get deeper into these recovery programs, a stronger sense of vocation and commitment to their degrees follows suit, the ATQ found. They become focused on different priorities, like classes, extracurriculars and work.

Whitney attributes the CRC’s success to a wide variety of programs: peer support groups, 12-step meetings, SMART recovery meetings, family and friends support meetings and the Residence of Addiction Recovery (ROAR).

“It’s an amazing thing, how this is a population of students that ordinarily struggle tremendously in college, but then with proper support, they tend to outperform the typical student,” Whitney said. “If you have even a dozen friends who are also in recovery, that really often turns the volume down on all of the social pressures to drink and do drugs.”

Whitney said the CRC differs from that of regular group recovery meetings — like Alcoholics Anonymous or Narcotics Anonymous — because students are surrounded by people living the same life they are: same campus, same social pressures, same age. A middle-aged person in group recovery settings may offer wisdom, but not that.

Students also relate more to their peers, which in turn makes them feel more seen. Students in recovery are often collectively working toward a brighter future, and hoping their recovery and academics will support that future, the ATQ study found.

“What students have at stake is their future,” Whitney said. “For older people, more of their life has been lived and more is known.”

While the CRC has its space in Pasquerilla Spiritual Center, this is a rarity among other universities. Whitney said many collegiate recovery programs struggle to find a space to definitively operate, which can weaken the sense of community important in students’ recovery.

The CRC’s warmly lit office provides constant access to this community, central to its success. In addition to its office, ROAR house’s founding in 2015 provides a 24/7 sober space and community.

Additionally, these programs tend to grow very slowly. Whitney said many students in their programs prefer anonymity to avoid taking on the stigmatized identity frequently associated with addiction and recovery; nobody wants to be somebody with a problem. As a result, it’s hard to get the CRC’s name out there for others to know it's there to join.

“It’s socially discrediting in various ways,” Whitney said. “But I would also argue that recovery, I mean, it’s not the worst status you can have.”

Julia Donato is currently a sober third-year studying psychology, but her story with addiction started young. By age 10, Donato was using drinking as a “way to escape.” Finding friends who normalized this in her teenage years made it easier to deny her alcoholism was a problem.

Because a lot of Penn State students drink, Donato said, she thought she would easily find another crowd that drank the same as her when she came to college.

Donato’s assumptions of what her new friends would be like soon proved false. She found her friends didn’t relate to her drinking seven days a week and regularly skipping classes.

“I crashed and burned quickly,” Donato said. “I didn’t make it through one semester up here. And then I got sober.”

When Donato returned to Penn State from rehab, she wanted nothing to do with the CRC. She thought it sounded “lame.” During her first semester back, her GPA was a 1.8.

Eventually, she attended one of the CRC’s weekly game nights. She now serves as Lions for Recovery’s president and lives in ROAR house.

She said her grades have improved every semester since she’s gotten more involved with the CRC. Her current GPA is 3.8.

“The CRC changed my life,” Donato said. “Having this community makes a lot of things easier.”

According to Whitney, there are currently around 17 students in the CRC, down from 29 in 2025. This fluctuation is common, as retention is made difficult by the very concept of college being a temporary space; students graduate, and by the time they join the CRC, they’re only there for around three semesters. Getting consistent activity in the program then becomes an issue too.

But this means students in the CRC are graduating at high rates. And even as it has to continuously replenish numbers, the small group means the program can serve them “very deeply.” It’s easier to allocate time and attention, and students have reported substantial, sustained mental health increases upon joining.

They tend to thrive in peer recovery rather than remain psychologically stagnant. However, mental health progress can remain out of reach for many students who haven’t first addressed their substance abuse; students whose mental health disorders co-occur with their substance use usually have to be treated for everything at the same time.

Noah, a third-year who requested anonymity due to fear of stigma, is one of those students. He said the CRC provided a “sigh of relief” when he came to University Park. Uninterested in the typical student experience of drinking, of going to bars, he was worried he’d be all alone.

Noah said he grew up with a stable family in a good home, and there weren’t any childhood signs that foreshadowed his addiction. But as he got older, he started struggling with his mental health. Within this struggle, he recalls searching for anything to make him feel good about himself.

“I never felt like I belonged,” Noah said. “I could be in a room full of my friends, and I would still just feel this lonely feeling.”

At 14, he found his mother’s wine and drank a bottle and a half before throwing up in her bed. He remembers thinking being drunk felt amazing, that he was fixed, in a sense; that alcohol was a solution for that isolating feeling he hadn’t found any other way to shake.

“The feeling of friction between myself went away when I was high or drunk,” Noah said. “It would always feel bad when I wasn’t high.”

He said the longer he used, the more drugs to feel that same baseline of “okay.” He dropped out of high school. He watched several of his friends overdose. Toward the end of his addiction, opiates were his drug of choice.

Noah then hit a point where he realized he could either get clean or end up dead. By 17, he was in rehab focusing on sobriety, followed by his placement in a secondary treatment care facility.

There, he met people he could finally relate to. For the first time, he said he felt like he had a community and that his life was moving in an upward direction. He highlighted peer support as one of the most beneficial things in his recovery.

The fact that he was having fun with these other people in recovery, he said, gave him hope. This hope is what helped him stick through his first year of sobriety.

After getting his GED, working in restaurants for a couple of years and completing two years at Penn State Scranton, Noah came to main campus. At the time of his transfer, he was going through a “rough patch,” exacerbated by the worry that he wouldn’t find a sober community in State College.

The CRC turned this anxiety around.

“Having this group of young people with similar experiences and way of looking at life, it helped so much,” Noah said. “There’s a safe space to be myself and not mute myself.”

He’s now on the CRC’s planning committee and leads peer seminars. He said he enjoys the service work behind the community.

“I really feel like I’m part of something where I can help others, and also help myself,” Noah said. “Having this here was a huge help to my mental health.”

Though students aren’t at the CRC for incredibly long periods of time, they still return post-graduation. Alumni regularly attend events and keep in touch.

Sean, a 2025 graduate who requested anonymity due to fear of stigma, applied to Penn State after meeting Whitney and learning about the CRC. He said this was the only way he could see himself staying sober.

Having come from an Irish-Catholic family, drinking was a large part of his culture. He had his first drink around 14 and recalls blacking out almost every time he drank in high school.

“I grew up idolizing booze,” he said. “I never realized I was uncomfortable until I started doing drugs like alcohol and marijuana, and it just gave me comfort in my own skin.”

Once COVID hit his senior year, the consequences that came from getting caught drinking dissipated. At the same time, his addiction progressed. He said he spent a lot of time drinking alone in his basement, playing Fortnite.

“It’s exactly as glamorous as it sounds,” Sean said.

He chose his college, Santa Clara University, for two reasons: warm weather and legal weed. Friends came easy, even if they noticed he did more drugs than them. His mental health was rapidly declining, and he said he was extremely suicidal, even with therapy.

After a certain point, the drugs and alcohol stopped giving him peace of mind and became a mere necessity.

His addiction ended after a two week stretch on cocaine put him in a drug-induced psychosis. He was placed in a psych ward, then a longer term rehab where he began committing to sobriety. This is also where he got connected with Whitney.

Sean went to four or five meetings a week once he joined the CRC. He said peer support is exactly as it sounds: people helping people.

“I don’t know if I could have stayed sober without the CRC,” he said. “Penn State’s a very party-oriented school. Drinking, football games, tailgating. That’s kind of the culture. So to have that nook where I can not only be away from that, but also having fun, was the key thing for me.”

Between Santa Clara and Penn State, he had two very different college experiences. The former he described as one that burned to the ground. The latter was a place with nothing but growth and memories he still cherishes.

“Every single day I would go to the CRC room and hear other people’s experiences, and that always gave me so much joy and hope and peace of mind knowing that it’ll get better,” Sean said. “Not only to get through my own challenges, my own tribulations, but to then pass on the wisdom I had to newer people and continue that cycle of building each other up and letting each other know we’re not alone.”

He graduated from Penn State with a 4.0 GPA. Now getting his master’s degree in social work, he’s working as a neuropsychologist assistant.

Sean said what he gained at the CRC still sticks with him. He’s learned how to show up for life every day, whether the day is good or bad. He said he’s considering becoming a therapist and wants to work with people with substance abuse disorders.

“I wouldn’t be where I am today without the CRC,” Sean said.

Editor's note: This story is part of the Penn State Student Media Network’s (PSSMN) 2025–26 enterprise reporting project. This year’s topic is mental health. PSSMN is comprised of CommRadio, The Centre County Report, The Daily Collegian, The Lion and PSNtv. Read more of the series here.

Editor's note: This story is part of The Daily Collegian’s participation in the Solutions Journalism Network’s Student Media Challenge. This year’s cohort focused specifically on mental health. Solutions journalism reports not only on widely shared problems, but also on how individuals and communities are working to address them.

MORE PSSMN STORIES

title: Penn State men's basketball earns commitment from transfer guard Thomas Allard
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has made another addition to the backcourt via the transfer portal.

University of Alabama Huntsville transfer Thomas Allard has committed to the Nittany Lions, he announced.

University of Alabama Huntsville competes at the Division II level as part of the Gulf South Conference. Allard averaged 13.7 points, 4.9 rebounds and 3.5 assists per game across 32 appearances this past season.

Allard seems to slot in as a depth piece for Mike Rhoades and company, who have landed commitments from five transfers so far this offseason. Allard joins Davidson transfer Roberts Blums and Central Connecticut State transfer Jay Rodgers as guard additions, while the Nittany Lions are also set to sign Miami (Ohio) forward Brant Byers and Buffalo center Tim Oboh.

Allard has size for the position as he's listed at 6-foot-7 and 210 pounds. He has experience since he will be in his redshirt senior year in 2025-26 as well.

Prior to playing for the University of Alabama Huntsville, Allard was with Lincoln Memorial University, which is also a Division II school. He played in 43 games total with Lincoln Memorial before transferring to the University of Alabama Huntsville.

Allard now gets his first shot at the Division I level with Penn State, as he has one season of eligibility left.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field's Boh Ritchie earns Big Ten Freshman of the Week
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coming out of a stellar indoor season, freshman Boh Ritchie continues to climb.

Ritchie was named Big Ten Freshman of the Week after her contribution to Penn State's 4x800-meter victory at the Penn Relays on Saturday.

B1G weekend at the Penn Relays leads to B1G honors for Boh! 👏🔗https://t.co/9U4pQuPFuh#WeAre | #PSUTF pic.twitter.com/hdFzdjpAIG— Penn State Track & Field/Cross Country (@PennStateTFXC) April 29, 2026

The New Zealand native ran a 2:02.41 third leg to help give Hayley Kitching a big enough lead to win the race.

Ritchie competed at the World Athletics Championships in March after earning a spot in the NCAA Championships earlier that month.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey lands commitment from goaltender Crew Petty
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has added a goaltender to back up Josh Fleming.

Crew Petty, an 18-year-old goaltender, announced his commitment to Penn State via an Instagram post on Wednesday.

Petty tallied a 2.19 goals-against average .919 save percentage in 33 appearances with Gilmour Academy in the USHS-Prep League.

He stands 6-foot-4 and weighs 205 pounds entering his first season at the collegiate level.

The Kennewick, Washington, native will likely serve as Fleming's backup next season following Kevin Reidler's departure to the NHL.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling, NLWC earn numerous senior, U20 winners
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
The first part of the world championships kicked off over the weekend as the senior level and U20 US Open events took place.

Nittany Lion Wrestling Club members earned four championships, with three in the senior level and another one at the U20 level.

Here’s how the Penn State contingent of wrestlers fared at the US Open.

Senior level

Luke Lilledahl kicked things off for the Nittany Lions representatives at 57 kilograms. As the No. 2 seed, he rolled through two of his three matches, with a lower-scoring victory over Ohio State commit Antonio Mills, 3-1.

It was a collision course to the finals between Lilledahl and Spencer Lee. The two met once again in the finals and battled it out. While Lee led throughout the majority of the match, Lilledahl struck with a left headlock, allowing him to get the takedown, winning 5-4, advancing automatically to Final X.

Just like his weight class during his freshman campaign, Marcus Blaze competed in a loaded 61-kilogram bracket featuring familiar faces.

Blaze dominated his first two matchups, winning by 11-0 and 10-0 tech falls, before meeting up with former United States world team member Seth Gross. Blaze showcased his strength in freestyle, defeating Gross, 6-1.

On the other side of the bracket, Ohio State’s Ben Davino and Oklahoma State’s Jax Forrest met in the semifinals in a rematch of the 133-pound national championship bout. Davino got his revenge, setting up yet another match between Blaze and Davino.

With Blaze’s strengths, he took down Davino with a 4-1 decision, defeating Davino again in freestyle.

Like Lilledahl, Blaze advances to Final X, where he’ll wrestle for a chance at the senior level world championship team.

Nate Desmond competed at 65 kilograms alongside Kyison Garcia, with the weight translating to 143 pounds.

Desmond won his first bout by tech fall before falling to former Nittany Lion Beau Bartlett by an 11-0 tech fall. Desmond worked his way to two victories in the consolations before losing by tech fall to State College native Pierson Manville, ending Desmond’s run with a 3-2 record.

For Garcia, his first matchup wasn’t as favorable. Garcia faced former Penn Stater David Evans, who ended his career at Utah Valley, and lost 11-0 in a little over five minutes. In the first round of consolations, Garcia earned a 10-0 tech fall before losing to Evan Potter of Southern Oregon RTC. The Nittany Lion ended with a 1-2 record at 65 kilograms.

Tyler Kasak continued his freestyle year, competing at 70 kilograms alongside Connor Pierce.

Kasak recorded three victories before facing Virginia Tech’s Caleb Henson. Kasak had to injury default out of the match and tournament at the 1:20 mark.

Pierce began his weekend with two straight tech falls before falling to Ian Parker, 15-8. Just like in the championship part of the bracket, Pierce earned two straight high-scoring victories in the consolations before falling by tech fall to end his tournament.

At 74 kilograms, Joe Sealey proved himself as a reliable wrestler. Sealey scored 10 points in his first two matches, winning both, before falling to eventual winner James Green by a 3-2 decision. Sealey moved through the consolations, before taking down two-time NCAA champion David Carr in the third-place bout.

After earning a U20 silver medal last year, William Henckel competed at 79 kilograms at the senior level this year. Henckel earned two straight tech falls, but then ran into Evan Wick, a former four-time All American. Henckel’s run ended with a 5-4 decision loss to Wick.

Henckel continued into the consolations, winning two matches including a disqualification victory over 2025 NCAA champion Dean Hamiti Jr. Henckel’s path came to an end with an 8-6 decision against former Iowa wrestler Patrick Kennedy.

Kyle Dake and Rocco Welsh both competed at 86 kilograms under the NLWC tag. As the top seed, Dake was out to prove he was still the top wrestler at the weight despite now being 35 years old.

Dake dominated with two-bonus point wins before wrestling two close matches, ultimately coming out on top, including a semifinal victory over Missouri’s Aeoden Sinclair. Dake ended up winning the weight class with an 8-2 decision over former Northern Iowa wrestler Parker Keckeisen.

Welsh pushed through his part of the bracket with two straight tech falls in under three minutes. After a 5-1 decision, he faced Keckeisen in the semifinals, losing to the runner-up, 11-4. Welsh entered the consolation bracket with a chance to finish in third, but lost two straight bouts to end his run with a sixth-place finish.

Josh Barr entered as the top seed at 92 kilograms. After winning the NCAA championship in March, Barr maintained that momentum with two straight tech falls. However, while dealing with an injury, Barr fell to the eventual winner of the bracket, Michael Macchiavello.

Barr had to medically forfeit out of the tournament, but will have another opportunity to make Final X at the World Team Trials.

Connor Mirasola began his weekend at 97 kilograms with a 10-0 tech fall. Despite falling in his next match, 6-2, he worked through the consolations with back-to-back decision victories to stay alive.

Connor lost to Oregon State’s Justin Rademacher, but took down Zane Lanham with a 12-1 tech fall in 3:47 to earn fifth place.

Cole Mirasola had a similar start to his run as his brother at 125 kilograms. He began with a 7-4 decision before falling to Iowa State commit Coby Merrill by an 11-0 tech fall, unable to overcome the size differential.

Cole battled through consolations winning with a 9-2 decision and a 10-0 tech fall to improve to 3-1 on the weekend. Despite this, a 5-2 loss to Jordan Wood ended his weekend.

Lilledahl, Blaze and Dake all won their brackets earning auto bids to Final X, where they’ll join Levi Haines, who accepted his automatic advancement to Final X after earning the world spot last year.

U20

Five Penn State representatives competed at the U20 level over the weekend — two signees, two commits and a redshirt freshman found themselves across four different weight classes.

2027 commit Grey Burnett was the lightest of the group, wrestling as the top seed at 57 kilograms. Burnett rolled through his competition, netting consecutive shutout wins with a combined 19 points.

Burnett earned a 14-7 decision before a 3:34 fall propelled him into the semifinals. A 14-2 tech fall over Mills put him into a matchup with Cornell commit Isaiah Cortez. In a close bout between highly ranked recruits, Cortez took down Burnett with a 4-1 decision.

2026 signee Sam Herring and 2027 commit Clinton Shepherd both wrestled at 65 kilograms.

Herring earned four straight 10-0 tech falls, before facing Minnesota commit Tyler Dekraker. Herring and Dekraker competed in a highly offensive bout, with Dekraker advancing in a 12-10 decision.

Herring earned two straight wins in the consolation bracket before facing Dekraker again, losing to the future Golden Gopher for the second time, this time by a 6-5 decision, putting him in sixth place.

Shepherd wrestled with the NLWC, and dominated out the gates with three tech falls. After a 4-2 decision, Shepherd fell in the quarterfinals. Shepherd then had three straight wins before facing Lincoln Sledzianowski after losing by tech fall to him earlier in the tournament. Shepherd came out victorious because of an injury default, earning third place at the U20 level.

After earning U17 gold last year, Jayden James wrestled at the U20 level as he prepares to enter his first year at Penn State.

At 74 kilograms, James proved he’s above the rest of the competition. As the top seed, he worked to five straight 10-0 tech falls en route to a finals appearance.

James matched up with Michigan signee Haakon Peterson. The Nittany Lion signee took down the future Wolverine with a 9-1 decision, winning every match in his bracket by at least eight points.

The lone current Nittany Lion at the U20 level, Asher Cunningham wrestled at 79 kilograms, which translates to just over 174 pounds.

Cunningham cruised to the finals behind five-tech falls in six matches. His only non-tech fall was a 14-5 decision. Cunningham lost to Virginia Tech’s Ryan Burton in the finals in a 9-4 decision.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's soccer signs Belgian standout Marthe Hens
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coach Erica Dambach's squad is adding an international talent.

The Nittany Lions are adding Belgian forward Marthe Hens to their 2026 roster.

✍️signed, sealed, delivered.Happy Valley, join us in welcoming Marthe Hens to the #PSWS family💙🤍#WeAre pic.twitter.com/RdxyKCfJp2— Penn State Women’s Soccer (@PennStateWSOC) April 29, 2026

Hens brings strong international experience, working with the Belgian U16, U17 and U19 Youth National teams. Additionally, the forward is a two-time Belgian national champion with Club Brugge.

Through the 2026 season with the U17 and U19 national teams so far, Hens has registered 13 caps, four goals and one assist.

MORE SOCCER COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball cancels game against West Virginia due to weather
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Inclement weather has forced a change to Penn State's schedule.

The Nittany Lions will no longer host West Virginia at 6 p.m. Wednesday due to forecasted rain.

🚨Schedule Update 🚨Due to forecasted rain, tonight’s game against West Virginia has been canceled.The Nittany Lions take on Minnesota this weekend at Medlar Field at Lubrano Park.Stay tuned for some Dollar Dog news…#WeAre pic.twitter.com/qoRlTwzCk1— Penn State Baseball (@PennStateBASE) April 29, 2026

According to AccuWeather, State College is projected to have an 82% chance of rain when the first pitch was scheduled for Wednesday night.

Penn State is still set to host Minnesota for a three-game series this weekend at Medlar Field at Lubrano Park. Game 1 will start at 5:30 p.m. Friday, followed by Game 2 at 2 p.m. Saturday and Game 3 at 1 p.m. Sunday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Preview | No. 15 Penn State men’s lacrosse set to face No. 11 Maryland in Big Ten semifinals
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State secured a first-round bye in the Big Ten Tournament with a decisive victory over Rutgers in the regular-season finale at SHI Stadium, taking home a 16-8 win and a four-way share of the Big Ten regular-season title.

Following a week away from action, the second-seeded Nittany Lions are set to return to Piscataway, New Jersey, to face third-seeded Maryland for the Big Ten semifinals at 6 p.m. on Thursday.

For Penn State, a victory over Maryland gives the Nittany Lions a stronger case for an at-large bid in the NCAA Tournament. The Terrapins’ higher ranking in the USILA poll gives them a leg up on the situation, but anything less than a nail-biting defeat may present the opportunity to another team to slip in the bracket over last year’s national runner-ups.

Tale of the tape

Penn State got the better of the Terrapins the last time the teams met. It was the Big Ten opener and the annual Wear White game, where the crowd set an attendance record at Panzer Stadium to aid the Nittany Lions claim a 10-6 victory.

The blue and white currently post a 7-5 overall record and 3-2 in the Big Ten, with a pair of narrow defeats at the hands of Michigan and Johns Hopkins. Maryland posted nearly the same 2026 result, also showing a 7-5 overall record and 3-2 in the conference. In addition to the loss to Penn State, the Terrapins also fell to the Blue Jays by the same one-goal margin in the final game of the regular season.

Maryland is coming off a 13-10 win over the Scarlet Knights in the Big Ten quarterfinals on home turf. Therefore, both squads are heading into the semifinals with momentum over the hosts. That said, the Nittany Lions’ win over Rutgers was more convincing on the scoreboard.

Players to watch

Attackman Hunter Aquino has been on a hot streak since returning from injury in mid-April, scoring four goals and adding an assist against the Scarlet Knights. Despite only playing in seven games this season, Aquino ranks second on the team with 18 goals, only three behind Kyle Lehman’s team-high 21.

Jack Iannantuono plays alongside Aquino and Lehman at attack and leads the Nittany Lions with 32 points, split between 16 goals and 16 assists. He was recently named the Big Ten Freshman of the Year, Penn State’s second in two years after following in Aquino’s footsteps.

Midfielder Andrew Beard also had a breakout performance against Rutgers, with two goals and three assists, despite ranking fourth among Penn State’s midfield offensive efforts.

Goaltender Preston Hawkins, who was recently named to the Big Ten All-Freshman Team after the regular season, had one of the best games of his budding collegiate career against the Terrapins, where he made 14 saves on 20 shots on goal. Against Rutgers, Hawkins made 10 saves on 16 shots, aiding the Nittany Lions in outscoring the Scarlet Knights 6-0 in the second quarter.

On the other side, Eric Spanos, despite having only played in nine games, is Maryland’s biggest offensive weapon. He tallied five points against Rutgers in the quarterfinals while making the most of his two scoring opportunities with a pair of goals.

On defense, sophomore Peter Laake leads the defense with 22 ground balls and 11 caused turnovers. He’s joined as a pole by long-stick midfielder AJ Larkin, who has scooped 38 ground balls and added seven caused turnovers.

Brian Ruppel is between the pipes for the Terrapins, posting a .528 save percentage and an 8.99 goals-against average. He’s on a two-game streak with double-digit saves, most recently making 11 saves on 21 shots against Rutgers.

MORE LACROSSE CONTENT

title: Senior Column | Future Nostalgia
author: Amelia Houck | The Daily Collegian
description:
Nearly four years ago today, I hid my face from my dad as I cried the entire way home from New Student Orientation. I was terrified about the prospect of change and starting college.

Four years later, I find myself still terrified about the prospect of change, but so incredibly grateful for the collegiate experience Penn State gave me.

Having the opportunity to attend school here and connect with so many friends, classmates and colleagues will truly be some of the most memorable years of my life.

In high school, as an avid creative writer, I wrote short stories and poems for my school’s literary magazine. Upon starting college, I knew I wanted to continue writing, but I just was not sure which organization I wanted to join.

At the end of my sophomore year, my mom encouraged me to apply for the Collegian. I began in summer 2024 as a news reporter and continued this position throughout the first semester of my junior year.

I met and had the ability to connect with so many people and organizations working in news, and I finally grasped a solid understanding of AP style for the first time. (Who would have thought!)

At the beginning of 2025 and my second semester of junior year, I had the opportunity to write an abroad column while studying abroad in Athens, Greece, for a History and Mediterranean studies program.

While abroad, I discovered how much I enjoyed writing a column and discussing my experiences. Never before did I have the opportunity to travel and try new foods, observe new cultures and make long-lasting friends.

I felt like writing gave me an outlet to express myself and I will forever be grateful to have had a position at the Collegian that allowed me to do so.

Beginning in my first semester of senior year, I transitioned to writing as a lifestyle blogger. I enjoyed writing about campus life, downtown restaurants, food spots, community events and coffee shops.

This position combined my love for interacting with others, which news reporting allowed me, while still allowing me some room for creativity and freedom that I had with my abroad column.

Being a part of the Collegian for these past three years has been nothing shy of the best decision I have made during my time at Penn State.

I will walk away and look back and know that the people I met and connected with, the stories and articles I have written, have helped shape who I am and where I am going.

So please, if your time at Penn State is not ending soon, soak in every second with your friends, family, and organizations you are a part of, because you will never truly know how much you will miss something until it is gone.

I’d like to thank all of my editors, Emma Naysmith, Nadya Solometo, Cearra O’Hern, Mercedes Hamilton, Kaitlyn Murphy and Katie Herron, for their wonderful advice, hardworking attitudes and support for me over the years.

To all of my roommates, I appreciate the genuine support you’ve shown me, from reading my articles weekly to your genuine interest in my story ideas. I love you all and appreciate your care for me.

I would like to thank my family, including my parents, siblings, grandparents, and aunts and uncles. Your time and consideration spent reading my stories and articles means the world to me. Your authentic, unconditional support of me makes me proud to continue writing, as I know at least one of you is reading my work.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Column | Is campus poetry dead?
author: Jadzia Santiago | The Daily Collegian
description:
Well, folks, it's finally here: this column will be published on the day of my final meeting as outgoing president of Writers Organizing to Represent Diverse Stories (W.O.R.D.S), the slam poetry organization here on campus.

I’ve been in this position for about three years — most of my undergraduate career. So, you might be wondering what I’ve gathered from being on the front lines fighting for poetry.

In short: we’re screwed.

Kidding! However, I’m sure it’s no surprise that I can report low attendance at events and relatively bare sign-up sheets.

It follows that there’s a reason for that, but after discussing it ad nauseam with my members, we’ve realized that there isn’t just one.

To acknowledge my own limitations, it could partially be a Penn State or college problem. From my conversations with other organization leaders, I’ve learned that interest in student organizations (specifically ones that don’t sparkle on a resumé) is declining on campus.

COVID-19 also killed a lot of in-person participation, and in a political landscape that’s been devolving since then, I speculate that a lot of us are too tired or afraid to put ourselves out there — especially to express our diverse stories and opinions.

But leaders of other poetry organizations and I say it’s also an arts-specific decline, which is (in my opinion) multi-faceted.

First, I’ll point at the cringe culture plaguing Gen Z (again).

I know, groan. There have been many inaccurate “kids these days” accusations levied against us, but I’ll say that it’s correct that we worship two things: conformity and the almighty dollar.

And let’s be real, poetry, specifically slam poetry, fits all the social panopticon’s parameters for Cringe. It’s self-expressive, bold and at times intimate.

The human soul is inherently unphotogenic, so imagine exposing parts of it every week — the horror!

What can I say? We’re products of our time.

Struggles for the arts, though, aren’t new or Gen Z-exclusive. At the very least, arguments about the financial viability of creativity have been going on for decades, and there have always been swaths of people terrified of non-conformity.

Additionally, the National Endowment for the Arts’ 2017-2022 Survey of Public Participation in the Arts found a statistically significant decline in engagement with literature (including poetry) in both younger (18-24) and older (55-75+) age groups.

So, safe to say lots of people are paying less mind to poetry, for multiple reasons.

That said, I don’t think it’s going away — far from it. But after leading a space like W.O.R.D.S., I can say that campus poetry spaces are struggling, and it all boils down to only small percentages of students thinking they’re important.

But what about that small percentage?

I’ve been groaning about low attendance, but those who do show up give me hope that’s helped me see this through. They’re passionate about poetry, eager to grow and self-express and remain unafraid of … well, everything.

Every time they show up, they show me that, despite the decline, the interest in poetry is still there.

If W.O.R.D.S. were to disband, like we considered doing a few times, many of those individuals might have had nowhere on campus to go. The decline of poetry spaces in a world focused on membership numbers means that we not only risk missing many poets, but also losing the ones we have.

That, to me, is tragic.

I love W.O.R.D.S. and groups like it, and I’ll say (despite the risk of being skewered for it) that I believe they’re crucial for young writers, who have voices and stories we need to hear.

You want Gen Z to save the world? Start by letting us write.

Perhaps I’m poisoned by the slam poets. Maybe I’m youthfully naive when I say that poetry should be accessible to everyone. While not everyone has high earning potential, they have something worthwhile to say and should have a place to say it.

It’s also possible I’m daft for believing that poetry can still impact anything. I mean, I’ve been staring down the practical obstacles for three years. I know that they’re all bigger than me (and a little scary).

I don’t care.

I’ve seen poetry change lives for the better, even just the lives of those passionate few. Their increased literacy, engagement with literature and confidence to be heard in an increasingly hostile world has made every single effort worth it.

So, what can we do? Unfortunately, these three years haven’t given me a solid answer.

But, if you haven’t already, I’m asking you, no matter who you are or what your role is on this campus, to join us.

Students, you don’t have to sacrifice an evening a week to a W.O.R.D.S. meeting in the name of the good fight, but be supportive of your writer friends. Resist the urge to cringe at the mistakes they may make or the genuineness of it all.

In my experience, it’ll bring you closer, or maybe even inspire you

Also, this is demonstrably a multi-generational issue. Everyone needs to start taking steps to improve the world that all generations inhabit, and engaging with literature and uplifting writers are crucial parts of that.

Keep supporting the arts and push back against the profit-focused world. Redefine importance and work toward making this planet safer for all. And most of all, don’t forget about those passionate few.

I want to write about a better world, and despite everything, I don’t think I’m alone in that.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Summer internship outfits that won’t stress you out
author: Lexi Cannan | The Daily Collegian
description:
Starting a summer internship sounds great until it’s your first morning and your entire closet suddenly looks like it belongs to a different person.

Nothing humbles you faster than realizing your usual rotation of hoodies, oversized T-shirts and going-out tops doesn’t translate to “professional.” One minute you’re dressing for class, the next you have to look like you have a LinkedIn profile and know how to use it.

The good news is you don’t need a whole new closet. You just need a few pieces that make it look like you have your life together, even if you hit snooze three times and ate a granola bar in the car.

Here are a few easy pieces that will make getting dressed way less stressful.

Trousers that do all the work

A good pair of trousers is basically your personality for the summer. Black, navy or beige — pick one and suddenly you’re “office girl.”

They do 90% of the work for you. You could throw them on with a random top you found crumpled on your chair, and somehow still look like you belong in a meeting. It’s actually kind of concerning how powerful they are.

They also give off the vibe that you understand emails and maybe even Excel. Meanwhile, you’re just hoping no one asks you what “circling back” means.

Best part? You can wear the same pair all week and no one will notice. It’s not lazy, it’s strategic.

Tops that require zero thinking

Mornings are not the time to be making big fashion decisions. You need tops you can grab half-asleep and not regret immediately.

Plain tanks, basic tees or simple blouses are perfect — nothing complicated. Aim for items that don’t need to be tucked in a specific way or adjusted every five seconds. If it requires effort, it’s already too much.

You also want tops that survive real life. Sitting all day, eating lunch, running to meetings — no outfit malfunctions allowed. The goal is to forget what you’re wearing entirely.

Having a few neutral options makes everything easier. You can mix them with the same pair of trousers all week and suddenly you look like you planned your outfits in advance, which you definitely did not.

A blazer for instant effort

A blazer is the ultimate lie. It tells everyone you have your life together when you absolutely don’t. You could be wearing the most basic outfit possible, but the second you throw on a blazer, it’s giving “I know what I’m doing,” even if you just asked someone how to log into the Wi-Fi.

It also saves you when the office AC feels like a science experiment. You walk in dressed for summer and immediately regret everything. The blazer steps in like a hero.

Plus, something about it makes you feel more confident, even if you’re still trying to figure out where the bathroom is.

Shoes you can actually walk in

This is where people make bad decisions. Don’t pick shoes just because they’re cute. You’ll be walking, standing, commuting and possibly speed-walking because you’re late.

Flats, loafers or cute sneakers (if allowed) are the safest options. If you’re thinking, “These might hurt but I’ll be fine,” you probably won’t be. By noon, you’ll be uncomfortable. By 3 p.m., you’ll be rethinking every decision you’ve ever made.

Comfortable shoes equal a better mood, and a better mood equals a better intern. That’s basically science.

A bag that holds your entire life

Your bag becomes your entire emotional support system. It needs to hold everything — laptop, charger, snacks, water bottle, lip balm, random receipts and probably your dignity at some point.

A tote or structured bag keeps everything together, even if the inside is absolute chaos. No one needs to know your bag is basically a junk drawer. From the outside, it looks organized and that’s what matters.

If you’re trying to find pieces that fit this vibe without spiraling in a dressing room, Harpers has options that make you look put together without trying too hard.

At the end of the day

Dressing for an internship feels stressful at first because no one actually explains what “business casual” means. Everyone just pretends they get it.

But once you find a few outfits that work, it gets way easier. You stop overthinking and just rotate the same pieces like everyone else. No one expects a fashion show, they just expect you to show up, do your work and look somewhat put together.

And honestly, if you leave the house on time — in real pants and wearing shoes that don’t hurt — that’s already a huge win.

MORE BLOGS

title: ‘It’s such a special place’ | Women’s lacrosse parents reflect on time at Penn State
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the last four years, David Ho, Beth Golder — the parents of Erika Ho and Maggie Driver — and other parents of Penn State women’s lacrosse’s senior class have spent weekend after weekend at Panzer Stadium in the spring.

On April 16, they traveled to Panzer Stadium for the last time, watching their daughters play the final home game of their collegiate careers.

“That makes me cry,” Ho told The Daily Collegian. “It’s been such a wonderful experience to watch her grow as a person and a player.”

While the families have officially been united since their daughters joined Penn State their freshman years, they’ve known each other much longer through connections in the lacrosse world.

“We are connected in so many ways,” Golder said. “When your child plays at this elite level, you’ve pretty much dedicated your whole life. It’s the hotels, the summers when they’re little, the club teams. We’ve pretty much all had the same journey.”

When the then-freshmen parents arrived on campus four years ago, they were welcomed with a beginning-of-the-season barbecue and an opportunity to meet the parents who’ve been there the last few years.

Among the interactions and greetings was an opportunity to learn the traditions of the Penn State program, including the cheers and expectations on gameday.

“This extended family that’s been together for the last four years has just been so amazing,” Ho said. “As a freshman parent, you show up on the sidelines during fall competitions and you’re lost; you don’t know what to expect. The parents were just so easy to show the ropes, like this is what expectations are during our games.”

Throughout the season, the parents have traveled to almost every home and away game, filling the bleachers.

While their cheering may not be heard or comprehended because of all the other noise on the field, they’ve made it a point to show up and stand out regardless.

“I don’t think they really listen to what goes on,” Ho said. “I think they know our presence of us being there and that we’ve got their back, whether win or lose, that we still love them and at the end of the day, they’re still Nittany Lions.”

While each family has had a unique journey to Penn State and during their four years on the team, Golder has experienced her daughter grow to be one of the best defenders in the Big Ten, ending her career with First Team All-Big Ten honors.

“It’s incredibly special,” Golder said. “She’s been playing lacrosse since kindergarten. Penn State was her dream school, and to see that come true, it’s been an incredible experience. We’re a Penn State family.”

Over the past four years, the team has experienced its fair share of highs and lows, from a pair of NCAA Tournament appearances to a losing record in 2025.

This season, the team welcomed new head coach Kayla Treanor, who has embraced the support from the parents and been grateful for everything they do for the program.

“They are just an incredible group,” Treanor told the Collegian. “I think that’s a big part of why we’ve had success as a team this year.”

Over the last several years, various parents have stepped up to take charge and lead the group, ensuring the traditions continue as they have in the past.

While the Spilker family doesn’t have a senior on Penn State’s squad this year, they’ve been a continuous mentor to younger families, having been with the Nittany Lions for 11 of the past 13 years with three daughters playing on the team.

“The Spilker family has been spectacular,” Ho said. “They’re really a true foundation of, ‘Hey, this is what goes on, this is what to expect.’ They’ve been really good mentors to follow.”

Various duties are split among the senior parents — currently with seven seniors — to ensure game day is successful.

Among the duties is waving a Penn State flag and hitting a cowbell after every goal, which is currently done by Kevin Kelly — Emma Kelly’s dad — and Golder.

“We’ve always made a point to support one another and to support everybody’s kids,” Golder said. “I think that’s what makes our team so special. I’m the bell ringer for our team, and my child’s a defender, so I never get to ring the bell for my kid since she doesn’t score. It’s so neat that every time I ring that bell, I’m ringing it for somebody else’s kid.”

Between the traditions, care and support from the parents toward the team, Treanor is grateful for the actions of the parents and what they’ve done for Penn State.

“In a lot of ways, they are in our locker room, whatever their messaging is to their daughter is how the kids feel,” Treanor said. “Across the board, we’ve got parents who are really supportive and are bought into this culture of what is best for the team and team first.”

With the regular season finished and a spot in the NCAA Tournament looming until Sunday’s selection show, the senior parents’ time is almost done at Penn State.

“(Penn State) is such a special place,” Ho said. “I’m gonna miss it so much.”

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State suspends Delta Chi Fraternity for hazing
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's chapter of Delta Chi was placed on indefinite suspension for a minimum of four years, effective April 14, after a university investigation into hazing allegations, according to a statement on the Penn State Student Affairs website.

The Penn State Office of Student Accountability and Conflict started an investigation in January of 2026 after they had received reports of alleged physical and mental abuse, sleep deprivation and excessive alcohol intake. It was placed on interim suspension while the investigation was ongoing.

After the four year suspension, Delta Chi may be allowed to return back to campus but may be placed on two years of conduct probation.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse star Rocquette Allen named Big Ten Freshman of the Year
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After earning six Big Ten Freshman of the Week honors throughout the season, Rocquette Allen’s standout season has paid off.

Allen was named the Big Ten Freshman of the Year on Tuesday after earning a unanimous selection to the Big Ten All-Freshman Team last week.

Rocquette Allen rockin' Freshman of the Year! 🎸 #B1GWLax pic.twitter.com/CafKaadPYv— Big Ten Lacrosse (@B1GLacrosse) April 28, 2026

The Lonetree, Colorado, native finished off her first regular season with the Nittany Lions with 45 goals and 22 assists, tied for the most goals scored on Penn State.

Throughout the year, Allen scored six hat tricks, including four five-goal games.

The freshman star kicked off the season with back-to-back five-goal performances in Games 2 and 3 against UMass and Bucknell, before scoring the game-winning goal in overtime against Pitt.

“This year alone she’s had a lot of success,” coach Kayla Treanor said. “Knowing Roc, it’s only going to continue to climb. Just when you think that’s it for her, there’s always going to be something.”

Ten days later, Allen scored her third hat trick in just her sixth collegiate game. A little over a month later, that high-scoring success continued with a near-upset over Maryland, where she scored five of the team’s 13 goals.

Under Thursday night lights against Oregon, Allen put up her final five-goal game of the season, before closing out the regular season with a hat trick against Rutgers.

“At the end of the day, there’s more to give and more to prove,” Allen said.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball freshman Allison Oneacre named top-ten third baseman by Softball America
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State Softball Allison Oneacre is a top-10 third baseman.

ao stayin in the spotlight.#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/Ex3pKOZpZW— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) April 28, 2026

Penn State has had a record-breaking season so far this year. With the new addition of freshman talent to the squad, Allison Oneacre has made her first year one to remember.

Softball America announced Tuesday afternoon its top ranks for third basemen. The Alpharetta, Georgia, native was ranked No.6 in the country.

Having one of the best freshman seasons in Penn State history, Oneacre has been unstoppable since putting on the blue and white jersey.

In her rookie season, Oneacre has a .0974 fielding percentage and a .388 batting average.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Weekly recap | Penn State men’s volleyball comes up big in EIVA Tournament
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Last week, Penn State looked to take home its fourth-straight EIVA Tournament title.

On Wednesday, the EIVA Tournament began from Rec Hall, as the blue and white earned the No. 1-seed and hosting rights to the tournament.

Granted a first-round bye, the Nittany Lions played their first game of the tournament in the semifinal round against Charleston on Thursday. The five-set victory propelled them to the EIVA Championship on Saturday.

Facing the No. 6-seed Princeton squad, the Nittany Lions were able to earn the EIVA Tournament title after sweeping the Tigers 3-0.

Here’s a look at what happened last week in the EIVA Tournament.

Offense-heavy performance

The blue and white looked calm and composed on offense throughout the entirety of the EIVA Championship match, and played a solid offensive game in the semifinal, too.

While it took the blue and white some time to get into motion against Charleston, once it did, the offensive pressure was on.

Sean Harvey had 19 kills in Thursday’s match. This marked his seventh straight game with double-digit kills. The EIVA Player of the Year also tallied a service ace, finishing the match with a total of 21.5 points.

In the EIVA Championship, the redshirt sophomore had 12 kills, which tallied his eighth straight game with double-digit kills. He also had another service ace with zero service errors on the night.

Harvey wasn’t the only Nittany Lions that performed well offensively over the week, though.

Tristan Hassell had a strong game against the Golden Eagles in the semifinal. The freshman had 16 kills and hit .448 on Thursday. He totaled a massive 19 points and created a major impact in the five-set victory.

In total, the blue and white had 60 kills to 18 attack errors in the semifinal match. Even more remarkably, the EIVA Championship match saw 42 kills from the Nittany Lions, with only 9 attack errors, hitting a collective .398.

Lots of digs

While offense was a standout in both matches of the EIVA Tournament, no games can be won without equally as strong defense. And part of that defense comes from digs.

As always, Ryan Merk was a focal point for the blue and white’s defense. The libero had seven digs in the semifinal and 13 digs in the championship match.

Merk finished Thursday’s game in a three-way tie for the most digs on the Penn State squad. Bennett Wilson also marked seven digs, as did Harvey.

As a whole, the Nittany Lions had a total of 29 digs against Charleston and 35 digs against Princeton.

Strengths in blocking

While digs are important, blocks are just as critical to stopping the opposing attack.

In the semifinal against the Golden Eagles, Owen Rose tallied five blocks. He totaled the same amount in the championship match.

Rose has had a great season as a middle blocker. Currently ranked No. 1 in blocks in the NCAA, the redshirt senior has been consistently reliable for the Nittany Lions.

Jaxon Herr and Hassell also had solid blocking performances against the Golden Eagles in the semifinal, with each putting up three blocks.

Herr had an additional three on Saturday against the Tigers, too.

Across the board, it seemed the blue and white came ready to battle on both sides of the ball last week.

During Selection Sunday, it was announced that the blue and white are set to face UC Irvine in the first round of the NCAA Tournament. The match will be at 9 p.m. on Friday at Pauley Pavilion in California.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Column | So long Barcelona, hello Chick-fil-A
author: Daniela White | The Daily Collegian
description:
After my first week in Barcelona, I knew I would never want to leave. After my first month in the city, I loathed how fast time was moving; how quickly each day began and ended. But now that I have three days left in this beautiful city, I have never wanted to be home more now than ever.

Putting that thought into writing, however, fills me with an insurmountable amount of guilt. Why on earth would I want to go back to my quiet hometown where I’d be bored out of my mind, instead of hoping to stay here for just one more day longer? Well, here’s my answer: I am so out of my mind exhausted, and I want Chick-fil-A.

After four months of traveling nearly every weekend, drinking and clubbing until 5 a.m. at least twice a week and blowing through all the money in my savings account, I need a goddamn break. I need at least 48 hours in my room at home, in quiet solitude with nothing but Netflix shows and American fast food to keep me company.

After living with six girls for four months in a four-bedroom apartment, as opposed to four roommates in Penn State in a two-story apartment, I need a break from civilization and I need good, greasy calories.

And while eating tapas and drooling over the paella in Spain has been a great hallmark of my time abroad, I desperately need all the food I’ve been missing from home. Chick-fil-A, Chipotle, KFC (they have it here in Spain but it’s nowhere near the same), Moby Dick (a kebab restaurant in Maryland), Starbucks’ grilled cheese and Pink Drink, Kung Fu Tea, and plenty more. If I had a dollar every time I thought about any of these places, I’d be able to afford another weekend trip in Europe.

For my final days here, while lounging at the beach or walking along the beautiful streets alight with spring’s warm sun, I’ve been looking forward to the drive home from the airport when I arrive back in America. I’ve been looking forward to hugging my dog, stealing some food from the pantry and binge-watching movies in my room. Just having these thoughts makes me so guilty yet excited, but I learned I need to give myself grace.

I’ve been on a strict schedule of partying until the sun rises and hopping on flights at 4 a.m. for months now, and not only my mind, fizzled out with balancing classes, writing columns, applying to internships, keeping up friendships here and in the states and traveling on top of all of that, but my body too is exhausted.

The days I usually wake up around 9 a.m. have slowly turned to noon, my eyes fighting to stay awake as the day is already halfway finished for some. Sleeping here makes me feel even more guilty, like I’m wasting all my precious time here to catch another hour of sleep instead of walking around.

I’m in a constant battle of soaking up everything in Barcelona while making sure I keep myself sane and healthy. So yeah, I think a couple days in my childhood room would do me some good.

I also feel like no abroad student would want to admit all of this, but at this point, I don’t care. If I could suddenly transport home with a Chick-fil-A eight-piece chicken nugget meal with medium fries and a Diet Coke placed in front of me instead of spending another day here, it would be the easiest decision of my life.

I have loved my time in Barcelona so immensely. I have learned so much about the city and myself, and I know I’ll be begging to come back in just a few weeks. I know I’ll forever be changed, but I know that my time here has come to a bittersweet end.

I knew that the day would come for me to take one last Uber ride to the Barcelona El-Prat airport, and it just so happens that I want that to take place just a couple of days earlier.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Weekly recap | Penn State softball continues historic 2026 season
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State had a successful week on the diamond, as it is in the final stretch of the 2026 season.

The Nittany Lions faced off against Saint Francis for their last nonconference game of the regular season on Tuesday, finishing with a rule-run victory. Securing a dominant 12-0 win in five innings, the blue and white tallied 10 runs in the bottom of the second.

Heading to Champaign, Illinois, to face off in its final road series of conference play, Penn State went up against the Fighting Illini for a Big Ten weekend.

Leaving Illinois victorious, as the blue and white were able to secure its third conference sweep of the 2026 campaign.

Within the past week, the Nittany Lions 2026 squad has stamped their names in the Penn State history books. Here is an inside look of what went right for the blue and white as it is close to the end of the regular season.

A record-breaking team

The 2026 softball season has been one of the best in Penn State history, and this past week the Nittany Lions continued to prove it.

Shortly after its run-rule victory against the Red Flash, the blue and white are currently ranked No. 1 in the nation in walks, as the squad has surpassed 250 walks drawn this season.

Penn State’s star freshman infielder Allison Oneacre was named a NFCA National Freshman of the Year top-25 finalist. The Alpharetta, Georgia, native has had a phenomenal season in the blue and white jersey, as she is just shy of a .400 batting average on the year.

Just before its series finale at Eichelberger Field, the squad continued marking its names in the history books by being the highest-scoring offense and owning the single-season RBI record, both surpassing 300 this year.

Closing their weekend against the Fighting Illini, three home runs in the series finale tied the record, then ultimately surpassed the single-season mark for homers. The 2026 squad set a new record of home runs with 76 balls out of the park so far this campaign.

Top Nittany Lions

It is no lie that the Penn State roster has a lot of young talent, as the 2025 signing class has been the biggest playmakers. It’s also been proven that the Nittany Lion lineup can produce strong appearances in the circle and at the plate.

Freshman outfielder Breanna Hanik had a time in her home state over the weekend. The Oak Lawn, Illinois, native launched a home run in every single game against the Fighting Illini.

Hanik has been productive at the plate, launching a two-run homer in the series opener and a three-run homer in the finale.

Oneacre continues to creep into the blue and white record books. The infielder’s performance Saturday afternoon proved just that, as she smashed her 17th home run of the season, while going 3-for-4 in the batter’s box with two RBIs.

The dominant performance from the freshman class didn't end there, as pitchers Brevyn Kellepouris and Mackenzie Duncan played their roles well in the circle.

Kellepouris got the start for Saturday’s game, as she pitched 2.1 innings and was able to keep the scoreboard clean by striking out an Illinois batter.

Duncan came in relief, pitching 3.2 innings, sending three Fighting Illini back to the bench, allowing only a single hit. The Winter Garden, Florida, native was not only able to keep a clean scoreboard to close out the game, but was able to keep zeros in the walk and run columns.

Making her return in the final stretch of the season, junior infielder Kaylie Walters appeared and quickly got back into the groove on both sides of the ball.

Walters was a force to be reckoned with Friday, as she went 3-for-4 at the plate with four assists, three putouts and a run scored. Making her return, starting in all four games this past week, the Philadelphia native had a flawless performance at shortstop.

The blue and white have gone 4-0 in the last week, making its overall record on the season 32-17 and 11-10 in Big Ten play. With a current four-game winning streak, the Nittany Lions are set to return to Happy Valley to host No. 4 Nebraska to close out the regular series.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: 2026 NFL Draft takeaways | What to make of all 8 former Penn Staters drafted
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Twenty former Nittany Lions declared for the NFL draft after a 7-6 season, but just eight of those former Penn State players heard their name called during the draft in Pittsburgh.

Despite multiple players sliding from where they were expected to be selected, streaks continued for the Nittany Lions, as it marked the ninth straight year with Penn State having at least two draft picks in the first two days.

It was also the fifth time in the last six years that at least eight players were selected in the draft, showcasing the overall depth of the team’s draftees this year.

Here’s a look at what’s next for all eight of the former Nittany Lions drafted in the 2026 NFL Draft.

Vega Ioane, Baltimore Ravens, 1st round

The first former Penn Stater off the board and the first interior offensive linemen drafted, Vega Ioane was selected with the 14th overall pick by the Baltimore Ravens.

Ioane provided reliability at left guard for two straight years, starting in every game he played in during his final two seasons.

The former Nittany Lion will likely immediately slot in as the starting left guard for the Ravens as the team made an emphasis to add protection to Lamar Jackson. He’ll push out Andrew Vorhees who was slated to start there before Ioane was drafted.

Baltimore ranked toward the bottom in sacks allowed last season, and the addition of Ioane, who didn’t allow a single sack during his two years as a starter will add strong blocking behind a run-heavy offense and a growing passing offense with Jackson at the helm.

Drew Allar, Pittsburgh Steelers, 3rd round

Somewhat unexpectedly, Drew Allar was the second former Penn Stater selected in the draft.

Allar was selected with the No. 76 pick in the third round as the fourth quarterback off the board.

The quarterback position is still up in the air for the Pittsburgh Steelers as they await the decision of Aaron Rodgers. If Rodgers doesn’t return and ends up retiring, Allar could be in for a competition for the starting job with Mason Rudolph and Will Howard.

Allar is in a strong position with new head coach Mike McCarthy, who’s been key in the development of Rodgers with the Green Bay Packers and the growth of Dak Prescott during his short stint as the head coach of the Dallas Cowboys.

If Allar can develop under McCarthy he could carve out a strong role with a chance to be a long term starter for the Steelers.

Drew Shelton, Dallas Cowboys, 4th round

Drew Shelton heard his name called early on in the third day, being selected by the Dallas Cowboys with their first pick of the day at pick No. 112.

Shelton showed signs early in his career, playing as a fill-in during his freshman season. While he provided consistency on the blindside, he likely won’t see immediate playing time.

The former Nittany Lion will begin as a strong depth piece for the Cowboys, with opportunities to work his way into a starting role if they move around linemen to fill in for the underperformers.

Dani Dennis-Sutton, Green Bay Packers, 4th round

The biggest faller of any former Penn Stater was Dani Dennis-Sutton, but his destination was a strong one and a good fit for him.
