The Nittany Lions fought back harder and extended their lead by six points, with kills by Harvey, block solos from Luoma and multiple attack errors and one service error from the Tigers, 23-17.

The match ended with a kill by Luoma and an ultimate ending with a starting serve by Rose and a Princeton attack error by Engling, 25-18.

Penn State didn’t just win, it proved why it’s the pinnacle of the EIVA and finished its title run with authority. Now, the blue and white turn its focus toward a deeper NCAA tournament push.

Up next

The NCAA Tournament selection begins Sunday, as the Nittany Lions have an opportunity to receive a bid. The national postseason begins May 1.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State LB Dom DeLuca inks UDFA deal with Baltimore Ravens
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Dom DeLuca’s Cinderella run at Penn State is finally over.

DeLuca was signed as an undrafted free agent by the Baltimore Ravens, becoming the first Penn State walk-on to make the NFL since Chris Stoll in 2023. The news was reported by Arye Pulli.

The Pennsylvania native joined the Nittany Lions in 2021 and took an early role in special teams before transitioning to a starting linebacker role.

While DeLuca played in almost every game during the first four years, he became a full-time starter after Tony Rojas’ season-ending injury during the 2025 season. He tallied 164 tackles, 17.5 tackles for loss, 4.5 sacks, two forced fumbles and five interceptions throughout his career.

DeLuca’s biggest moment of his five-year Penn State career came in the first round of the 2024 College Football Playoff, where he recorded two interceptions, including one for a 23-yard touchdown.

Despite being a walk-on, DeLuca grew into a vital role within the Nittany Lions’ defense and was a three-year special teams captain.

“I'm really just trying to show them I can play,” DeLuca said at pro day. “I have the ability to play a lot of special teams and be reliable on defense when needed, just doing anything I could to make a team really and just be trying to be that guy.”

DeLuca willl reunite with Vega Ioane and Nick Dawkins in Baltimore.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State DT Jordan van den Berg drafted by Chicago Bears
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After three years at Penn State and another two at Georgia Tech, Jordan van den Berg is heading to the big leagues.

Van den Berg was drafted with the 213th pick in the sixth round of the 2026 NFL Draft by the Chicago Bears.

During his three seasons in Happy Valley, van den Berg played in 26 games, tallying 26 tackles, 2.5 sacks and four tackles for loss. He also forced a fumble in each of his first two seasons.

In 2024, the South Africa native transferred to Georgia Tech, where he saw an increased role from his earlier years.

Van den Berg started in seven games and played in six more during his fourth year, recording 23 tackles, five tackles for loss, a sack and a pair of forced fumbles. He earned All-ACC second team honors as a result of his stellar play.

He made the biggest impact for the Yellow Jackets in 2025 as he started in all 13 games. Van den Berg saw an increase in nearly all of his stats in his final year, notching 44 tackles, 11 tackles for loss and three sacks. He was named an All-America honorable mention by Phil Steeler and was an All-ACC first team selection.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State LS Tyler Duzansky signs with Las Vegas Raiders as UDFA
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s starting long snapper will be sending the football to professional kickers and punters.

Tyler Duzansky inked a deal with the Las Vegas Raiders as an undrafted free agent following the 2026 NFL Draft, per Kicker Update on X.

In his redshirt senior season, Duzansky was a finalist for the Patrick Mannelly Award, awarded to the top long snapper in the country. He helped punter Gabe Nwosu average 45.4 yards per punt in his first and only season as the No. 1 punter.

The previous year, Duzansky was named All-Big Ten third team, assisting Riley Thompson’s average 42.8 yards per punt en route to a College Football Playoff semifinals berth.

“As somebody with a position where you only get to go out there like 10 or 15 times, you definitely have to be a low-maintenance, high-production guy,” Duzansky said about speaking with teams. “As a snapper, if you mess up one time out of 10, you're getting cut.”

But he doesn’t just contribute to the kicks. Duzansky led the nation with six tackles as a long snapper in his redshirt sophomore season in 2023, using a blend of athleticism and strength with a build more reminiscent of a linebacker. He made two tackles in his final two seasons, both of which were solo.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State WR Trebor Peña signs with Jacksonville Jaguars
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
After spending his sixth collegiate season at Penn State, Trebor Peña has punched his ticket to the NFL.

Peña signed a contract with the Jacksonville Jaguars as an undrafted free agent following the 2026 NFL Draft, per Jordan Schultz.

The New Jersey native spent five years at Syracuse with an up-and-down career. He had a pair of season-ending injuries, first mid-season in 2021 later in the 2023 season opener.

Originally an elite return specialist, Peña bloomed into the No. 1 receiver role for the Orange as a redshirt senior and put up 941 receiving yards for nine touchdowns and was named All-ACC second team before transferring to Penn State the following year.

He didn’t find as much success in the Big Ten, however, accumulating 552 receiving yards for two touchdowns. Peña put a cap on his collegiate career with a career-long 73-yard touchdown in the Pinstripe Bowl victory over Clemson.

The 5-foot-10, 187-pounder also impressed at Penn State Pro Day, running a 4.52-second 40-yard dash and recording 19 reps on the bench press.

“We've been through a lot, but I feel like it was all worth it,” Peña said at pro day. “You learn a lot of lessons going through adversity and stuff that doesn't always go your way, but you have to find a way to bounce back. I feel like we all persevered and pushed through and showed the type of people we are.”

Peña’s versatility as a wideout and a return man gives him the ability to shine on either offense or special teams. His mechanics, catching in stride and lack of mistakes when the ball lands in his hands, offer Trevor Lawrence a reliable option through the air.

“Mental toughness, physical toughness, playmaking ability, all the things that you can do, I feel like you got to put that on display,” Peña said. “I feel like I'm a good receiver outside, inside, using the backfield, kick return, punt return — I feel like I could do it all.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State TE Khalil Dinkins inks deal with San Francisco 49ers
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s reputation as a tight end factory continues, as the Nittany Lions put a tight end through to the NFL for the fourth straight year.

Khalil Dinkins signed a deal with the San Francisco 49ers following the 2026 NFL Draft that includes $275,000 guranteed, per Tom Pelissero. He joins his father, Darnell, as the second player in his family to make it to the pros.

Dinkins played a more involved role in the run-blocking game, using his 6-foot-4, 251-pound build to bully defenders. However, when given the chance to receive, his reliability resulted in nearly 20% of his catches being taken to the end zone.

Dinkins’ catching capabilities were utilized more in the early-mid stages of his redshirt senior campaign, making 14 receptions for 167 yards through the first nine games. He didn’t record statistics in the final four games of the season, leaving the duties to Andrew Rappelyea and Luke Reynolds to close out the year.

As a redshirt junior, he sat behind current Indianapolis Colt Tyler Warren, resulting in a smaller role for Dinkins, who had 14 receptions for 122 yards on the season.

Dating back to his redshirt freshman season, Dinkins ended his collegiate career with seven touchdowns and 399 receiving yards.

He’ll be likely sitting behind George Kittle and Jake Tonges in the early stages of his career, with the potential to develop into a reliable backup option for the 49ers.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State OL Nick Dawkins signs with Baltimore Ravens as UDFA
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Nick Dawkins is adding to his family’s long lineage of pro athletes.

Dawkins signed with the Baltimore Ravens as an undrafted free agent after six seasons at Penn State, per Matt Zenitz of CBS Sports.

The center took over the starting job in 2024 after sitting behind Juice Scruggs and Hunter Nourzad for four years. Dawkins opted to use his sixth year of eligibility in 2025, giving him two years as the Nittany Lions’ starting center.

Dawkins became the program's first recipient of the Allstate Wuerffel Trophy in 2024, which is "presented to the FBS player who best combines exemplary community service with leadership achievement on and off the field" each year.

He has been voted a captain for Penn State the last two seasons and started each game during that span outside of the Pinstripe Bowl.

"I'm someone that values trust in relationships, and all I did, all I did in everything that I ever do, will always be for the people that I love and cherish," Dawkins said at pro day. "And it'll be for my parents, my sisters, my family and my teammates, and they're always in one group, so someone that cared about their teammates. That's all I really care about."

Dawkins will now look to try and earn a roster spot in Baltimore and live on his family's legacy of producing professional athletes. His father, Darryl, played in the NBA for 14 seasons, and he's the cousin of NFL Hall of Famer Brian Dawkins and current Buffalo Bill Dion Dawkins.

Dawkins will also reunite with fellow two-year starter Vega Ioane, who was drafted by the Ravens in the first round this year.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State WR Devonte Ross signs with Los Angeles Chargers
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After three seasons at Troy and a year at Penn State, Devonte Ross’ collegiate career is now behind him.

Ross signed as an undrafted free agent with the Los Angeles Chargers, per Jordan Schultz.

The Georgia native was an all-conference receiver and special teams player with the Trojans, tallying a career-high 1,043 yards and 11 touchdowns in his junior season.

While Ross looked to make the most of his final year transferring to a Power Four school, his senior season was ultimately underwhelming. He recorded just 36 receptions for 501 yards and five touchdowns.

Despite the statistical decline, Ross experienced several big moments, including catching the game-tying touchdown against Oregon in the White Out game.

Ross showed signs of success during the 2025 season on offense and special teams, averaging 13.9 yards per catch and leading the team with 71 yards on punt returns.

“It’s a blessing, man, just being in this position,” Ross said at Penn State Pro Day. “Growing up, I wouldn’t even say I saw myself being able to do something like this. Penn State was able to help me showcase my ability to the rest of the world.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State WR Harrison Wallace III inks deal with Arizona Cardinals
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After becoming a full-time starter for Penn State in 2024, Harrison Wallace III took his talents to Ole Miss, where he further put himself in a position to be an NFL wide receiver.

Wallace signed with the Arizona Cardinals as an undrafted free agent, per Jordan Schultz.

The Alabama native made a big jump in his career entering 2024, recording a career-best 117 yards and two touchdowns in the season opener against West Virginia. Throughout the season, he tallied a then-career-best 720 yards and four touchdowns.

Wallace was a heavy reception, short-yardage receiver. During his last year with the Nittany Lions, he caught 46 passes, but only averaged 3.1 yards per catch.

The downfall of Wallace’s Penn State career came in the Orange Bowl against Notre Dame, where he recorded -3 yards.

Wallace transferred to Ole Miss for his final season where he improved his stats with 61 receptions and 934 yards. The 6-foot-1 receiver experienced another College Football Playoff semifinal appearance with the Rebels, tallying four catches for 40 yards this time around.

His best performance with Ole Miss came in the Sugar Bowl against Georgia, where he recorded nine catches for 156 yards and a touchdown, marking his third 100-yard game of the season to help upset the Bulldogs.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State’s backup QBs take advantage of Blue-White Practice reps despite youthful struggles
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Earlier this week, Matt Campbell said at media that his younger quarterbacks have had youthful moments throughout the spring.

Those moments were on full display at Blue-White Practice on Saturday, as freshman Peyton Falzone threw an interception and Kase Evans sometimes looked uncomfortable.

Despite the struggles, Campbell believes with Rocco Becht limited and Alex Manske out, it’s important for his younger players to gain experience and earn live reps.

“Holistically, I think it will benefit greatly that some of those guys didn’t get those reps,” Campbell said. “Some of those young guys had to get reps and really have to grow forward from where we are today.”

On Saturday, the fans got their first look at Falzone, who was one of Penn State’s two commits on the initial National Signing Day in December. But Falzone put on a mistake-filled performance, throwing an interception to fellow freshman Josiah Zayas, who returned it nearly 50 yards for a touchdown.

“The biggest thing I wanted to show these guys is you’re going to mess up,” Becht said. “You’re gonna fail. So it’s just how you continue to get better, how you continue to bounce back in the classroom, in the film room, on the field, off the field.”

In addition to the woes by Falzone, Evans had times of struggle. While he didn’t throw an interception, Evans recorded what would’ve been a sack during the non-contact portion of 11-on-11 action.

With all six quarterbacks healthy, Evans is likely the last-resort option for the Nittany Lions. However, Saturday was another opportunity to get as many people acclimated as possible to Beaver Stadium in front of a small group of fans.

“To get Kase Evans in the game and see from small-town Texas to where he is today, it’s huge,” Campbell said.

When faced with pressure, Evans also appeared to struggle with decision making, tensing up during various moments throughout the practice.

While the two struggled on Saturday, veteran running back Carson Hansen has seen their growth throughout the spring as he’s lined up alongside them in the backfield.

“I’ve seen a lot of improvements from the early part of spring and them growing in the playbook and growing their knowledge throughout the rest of the spring,” Hansen said. “They’ve worked really hard and I just really appreciate them.”

Among the five quarterbacks who took the field today, redshirt senior Connor Barry — a transfer from Division III Christopher Newport — played the biggest role on the field.

While Barry scored two touchdowns, including a throw to tight end Brian Kortovich and a rushing touchdown, he had his fair share of struggles, throwing an interception during the 7-on-7 drives.

Veteran wide receiver Brett Eskildsen hasn’t seen live reps this spring as he recovers from injury, but saw the growth of the young quarterbacks throughout their first taste of collegiate football.

“It’s been great for them to come out here with the offense looking as good as they did in the rain, that’s super impressive to me,” wide receiver Brett Eskildsen said.

While Barry took leading quarterback responsibilities in 11-on-11, the two freshmen quarterbacks lined up under center to close out Blue-White Practice, earning some valuable reps in front of fans.

“I’m so proud of those guys and what they were able to do — they want to learn,” Becht said. “They always ask questions and I think for them, continue to ask questions, continuing to ask coaches, meet with them and learn the offense even more.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘We can fight through adversity’ | Penn State's chemistry much improved at conclusion of spring
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Blue-White Practice gave Penn State fans a glimpse at the future, with the Nittany Lions sporting a new roster lined with fresh faces across all position groups.

Many transferred from Iowa State, following the trail led by coach Matt Campbell, but they, along with those who stayed at Penn State or transferred in from elsewhere, have had new teammates to get accustomed to.

During the spring, the new and old Nittany Lions have made attempts to bridge connections with their teammates and coaches, leading to on-field chemistry. At the Blue-White Practice, some of those combinations were on full display.

“Coach Campbell has done a great job bringing us all together,” Hansen said. “A lot of different backgrounds, coming from a lot of different places. I think he's done a great job building that culture. And we've seen a lot of improvements in the leadership aspect of things, and he's done a great job, and he just keeps motivating us.”

Campbell has made it a point of emphasis from the beginning of his tenure for the entire team to come together as one. He and the coaching staff have been meeting with players individually about what they need to do to see how they align with the team’s direction.

That drive to see the team gel has had an impact on various position groups individually, as well as the feel of the entire locker room. It sparked the inspiration of leaders from across the roster, including Rocco Becht.

Becht has been recovering from surgeries to both of his shoulders over the course of the spring, and Campbell has reduced his throwing reps to help him ease back into action. However, Becht didn’t sit back and took an active role in building relationships and chemistry within the locker room.

“Building my chemistry in the film room and being a leader off the field for me was a huge step in my leadership role,” Becht said. “And so I thought I had a good spring to develop in that way. The chemistry has been able to build over the past couple weeks with me just getting into 7-on-7 with the receivers and tight ends, we've been able to get really good reps the past couple weeks.”

Becht was one of the first players to greet his new teammates when they all first arrived on campus, individually introducing himself to anyone that crossed his path, establishing a connection between him and those who play alongside him. He’s been a mentor in the ways that he’s been working closely with his teammates — almost like another coach — to aid their growth.

After the conclusion of the Blue-White Practice, Campbell said that the team’s chemistry is going to be how the Nittany Lions battle through tough moments of the “storm of a college football season.”

“We can fight through adversity, a bad quarter, a bad half, because all that stuff's coming,” Campbell said. “And the key to our success is are we together enough to be able to fight through hard. I think those are great growth opportunities for us, and we'll just have to continue to come throughas we work through the rest of the summer and certainly fall camp.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State RB Kaytron Allen drafted by Washington Commanders
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
The second half of Penn State’s dynamic running back duo from the last four years is heading to the NFL.

Kaytron Allen was selected 187th overall by the Washington Commanders in the sixth round of the 2026 NFL Draft, giving him a chance to return to his home state.

“I feel good to have this opportunity,” Allen said at Penn State Pro Day. “I’ve been dreaming of it for so long.”

Allen took a gamble following the 2024 season and announced his return to Penn State, where he went on to break records during his senior year.

The Virginia native set the all-time rushing yards record during his 160-yard game against Nebraska. Allen finished his career with 4,180 yards, surpassing the previous record of 3,932 yards set by Evan Royster in 2010.

Allen recorded touchdowns in all but one game during his senior season ran for over 100 yards in five different contests, including a season-high 226 yards in his final game against Rutgers.

Throughout his four-year career in Happy Valley, Allen and fellow running back Nick Singleton broke several records together.

In their first seasons at Penn State, the duo became the first true freshmen teammates in Big Ten history with over 700 rushing yards each in a single season. Fast forward to the end of their careers and they became the first players in Penn State history to reach 3,000 rushing yards at the same time.

Allen is now the fourth former Nittany Lion running back in the NFL, including Saquon Barkley, Miles Sanders and Singleton.

“Whatever happens, I’m gonna take advantage of it,” Allen said. “All I need is an opportunity to just take advantage of everything.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball dominates with run-rule win against Illinois
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
With a dominant win yesterday against the Fighting Illini, Penn State looks to build on its momentum and strengthen its chances of making the Big Ten Tournament. A win today would give the Nittany Lions a 2-0 lead in the series.

Penn State secured a run-rule 10-0 victory against Illinois.

Senior right-hander Karley Yergler starts in the circle for the Fighting Illini, allowing Kaylie Walters to get a walk and Breanna Hanik to get a two-run home run to get the blue and white going with a 2-0 lead.

Allison Oneacre kept it going with a drive down left field and got a single. With no outs yet, Brooke Klosowicz hit it midfield, but the Illinois defense quickly shut it down and cleared the bases.

Both Macy Chamberlin and Gabby Gradishar got a walk, with Natalie Lieto getting hit by a pitch, leaving the bases loaded for the blue and white.

Fighting Illini coach Trya Perry calls a timeout to reset the defense and with this, Yergler was able to strike Cara Bohner, ending the Nittany Lions' reign.

The Nittany Lions throw Freshman left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris into the circle to start the bottom of the first. But the defense was relentless as it allowed zero runs for the Fighting Illini.

Illinois responded with the same inning, allowing zero runs for the Nittany Lions to start the second.

Momentum started to pick up for the Fighting Illini as Ellie Haggard stepped to the plate and got a single. Jackie Yeager followed up with a hit to right field, but Haggard got out.

Gradishar then gets a shoestring catch to get Addy Kiddy out. Kellepouris struck out Brooke Stang, ending the second inning for Illinois.

Oneacre stepped to the plate and immediately secured a solo home run, putting the Nittany Lions up 3-0 to start the third. But the Illinois defense shuts down the rest of the Nittany Lions’ batters.

Nothing much for the Fighting Illini in the bottom of the third, except a few hits that resulted in outs. But Coach Clarisa Crowell switched pitchers and put in another freshman in Mackenzie Duncan.

The blue and white came out with energy to start the fourth with Bohner hitting it left-field and making it to first. Frankee Flesher comes in as a pinch runner for Bohner on first. Barabanente got hit by a pitch and walked to first.

Coach Perry relieves Yergler and puts in left-hander Abby Sabalaskey for Illinois.

With that, Hanik stepped to the plate and hit it to left field and made it to first. The Nittany Lions were on a roll. Oneacre grounded and made it to first. With an error by the shortstop, Barbanente scored and made the score 4-0, continuing the lead for the blue and white.

As the sun poses to be a struggle for both defenses, Yeager capitalizes on it and doubles to center field. Other than that, the fourth was quiet for the Fighting Illini, who hope to get on the board soon.

The fifth inning was quiet for both teams as no runs were scored.

The sixth inning is where the bats came alive for the blue and white as Barbanente hits it right field for a single. Hanik follows up with a hit infield and also makes it to first, giving the Nittany Lions momentum after a few silent innings.

Oneacre is on a roll as she stepped up to the plate and secured another hit and Barbanente scored on a fielder's choice, making the score 5-0.

In the sixth inning alone, the Nittany Lions scored five runs, making the score 10-0. With that, the blue and white broke the record for most runs in a single season with 345 runs. Marking an exciting day for the Nittany Lions.

Duncan closed out the game in six innings and secured a 10-0 run-rule victory for the Nittany Lions.

Up Next

The final game between Penn State and Illinois is set for 1 p.m. Sunday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State DT Zane Durant drafted by Buffalo Bills with last pick of 5th round
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After four years in the cold of State College, Zane Durant isn't catching a break from it yet.

Durant was selected as the newest member of the Buffalo Bills, being drafted with the 181st pick in the fifth round of the 2026 NFL Draft.

The former Nittany Lion was the seventh former Penn State player selected.

“It’s a blessing just to be in that conversation and get drafted,” Durant said at Penn State Pro Day. “I’m just grateful for the opportunity.”

Durant has been a full-time starter for the Nittany Lions since his sophomore year and tallied 89 tackles, 22 tackles for loss and 10 sacks across his career.

Despite a disappointing senior season after coming in with preseason All-America honors from several media outlets, Durant boosted his draft stock with a stellar performance at the NFL Scouting Combine.

At nearly 300 pounds, Durant ran the fastest 40-yard time among defensive tackles since 2023, clocking in at 4.75 seconds.

While he’s undersized, his speed was a bright spot during the 2025 season, earning sacks against top-rated Fernando Mendoza and speedy Nico Iamaleava.

In addition to his on-the-field talent, Durant also shined as a leader, being named one of five team captains in 2025.

Now, Durant will take his talents to Buffalo, where he’ll work for a spot on the defensive line.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State running backs continue growth as unified unit during Blue-White Practice
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Following the departure of Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen to the NFL, the running back room featured new and returning faces, all with a chance to make an impact heading into the Blue-White Practice.

While Ohio State transfer James Peoples and Iowa State transfer Carson Hansen received a bulk of the carries during the practice, each of the five running backs have grown into one singular unit, all encapsulating what it means to be a running back at Penn State.

“They’re what a Penn State running back is,” offensive lineman Cooper Cousins said. “They’re shifty, they’re smart. They set up our blocks. All those guys in that room are the definition of a Penn State running back.”

Some may see the top running back as a competition between all of them, the group itself sees the room as something that can benefit from each other, often helping one another out rather than focusing on their own individual growths.

“I think you see a lot of different running styles,” Hansen said. “We like to share tips with each other, you know, what can we work on? Our weaknesses, our gaps, it makes it a lot more special to come together as a group and just pick apart each other’s game to be able to just improve our game.”

Hansen had a similar style room at Iowa State, as he split carries with Abu Sama III last season. Similarly, James Peoples wasn’t the main running back during his two years at Ohio State, finishing third in rushing yards in 2025.

While the room has new faces, it shares the same style as previous running back rooms, knowing that each one of them can make each other better.

“They continue to work each and every single day,” Rocco Becht said. “Coach (Savon) Huggins has taught them right. “They’re physical, they’re downhill backs, and they can make people miss, so I’m excited to see that trio on the field come this season.”

Each running back has their strengths, providing a unit that could be tough to stop with multiple backs coming at opposing defenses at different times. Hansen is “going to hit the hole hard,” as Cousins put it, where Peoples is more shifty and quick, using his “Greek god” physique to his advantage.

While Martin didn’t receive as many reps as the other two running backs, he’s still likely to make an impact as someone who’s ran behind a Penn State offensive line before or out wide, as Martin said two weeks ago he’s playing more receiver.

The running back room has fully developed the camaraderie that is seen around the team, something that didn’t necessarily come easy, but the running backs have quickly grown into one unit.

“It's pretty special to have a family of running backs like that, no ego, and we're just there to humbly work hard,” Hansen said.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State RB Nick Singleton drafted in 5th round by Tennessee Titans
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s all-time touchdown record holder is headed to the NFL.

Nick Singleton was selected 165th overall by the Tennesee Titans in the fifth round of the 2026 NFL Draft.

During his time in Happy Valley, Singleton broke records across the board.

On his way to becoming the Big Ten Freshman of the Year, he set the all-time program record for rushing touchdowns as a freshman with 12. He rushed for 179 yards in his Beaver Stadium debut against Ohio on Sept. 10, 2022, a single-game record.

In the 2024 College Football Playoff semifinals against Notre Dame, Singleton scored three rushing touchdowns, tying the Orange Bowl record as the seventh player to do so.

At the end of his time in Happy Valley, Singleton scored 45 rushing touchdowns, 55 total touchdowns and had 5,586 all-purpose yards — all program records. Singleton claimed all three of the records in a 40-36 victory over Rutgers on Nov. 29, 2025, where he rushed for 86 yards and received for 23.

“Obviously it wasn't the season I wanted, such as for the team too, but it finished off strong,” Singleton said at pro day. “End up winning the (Rutgers) game and leave my legacy here at Penn State. It means a lot for me and the reason I came back too."

Only Singleton and current Philadelphia Eagle Saquon Barkley have achieved over 5,000 all-purpose yards and over 50 touchdowns in Penn State history, with Singleton being the first nationally to do so since Wisconsin's Jonathan Taylor.

Singleton broke his foot during Senior Bowl practice and had to undergo surgery ahead of the draft. He was medically cleared to run again on April 17 and has since resumed training although he missed out on both the Penn State Pro Day and the NFL Scouting Combine.

“I’ve just been practicing it the whole time, and just taking one meeting at a time,” Singleton said. “Just taking a deep breath and answering the questions, really just talking ball. So that's really the fun part about it. So I don't feel the pressure.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State S Zakee Wheatley drafted No. 151 overall by Carolina Panthers
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State has another safety selected in the NFL draft.

Zakee Wheatley was selected 151th overall by the Carolina Panthers in the fourth round of the 2026 NFL Draft.

Wheatley bet on himself, returning to Penn State for a fifth season, where he started all 12 regular season games. This came one year after he was deemed the “Turnover King,” making splash plays in the Nittany Lions’ run to the College Football Playoff Semifinals including earning defensive player of the game honors in the Fiesta Bowl.

The Maryland native was the focal point in the defensive back unit last season after Jaylen Reed and Kevin Winston Jr. left for the NFL following the 2024 season. Across Wheatley’s five seasons with the Nittany Lions, he was in a room with numerous now-NFL safeties. Those players helped Wheatley get to their position and now an NFL draft pick.

“Being a young player and having dudes like (Jaquan) Brisker, (Ji’Ayir Brown), Keaton (Ellis) and I can keep on going, but having those OG type guys in the room set a great example for me,” Wheatley said at pro day.

The versatility of Wheatley could play a major factor in his success at the next level. He started off as a cornerback and moved full time to safety, with his coverage ability becoming a strong suit in his game.

Versatility has become a difference maker in the NFL with the rise in safeties moving into the box and having to cover running backs and tight ends man-to-man, which could play into the strengths of the former Nittany Lion.

“I think it just shows I’m a versatile player,” Wheatley said. “Starting at corner, playing post safety and in this last year, going in the box, showing some blitzing, playing the run. I think it shows I can play the post, and I can also big up and then go play the run.”

After playing in every game during his redshirt freshman and redshirt sophomore season, Wheatley became a full time starter at safety in 2024, starting in 28 straight games.

Wheatley becomes the 10th Penn State safety drafted all-time. He’s the fifth safety drafted in the last four years as the room flourished under former safety coach Anthony Poindexter.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: 3 takeaways from Penn State's Blue-White Practice
author: Collegian football staff
description:
Penn State took to the field at Beaver Stadium for the first time in the Matt Campbell era for the Blue-White Practice on Saturday, a variation of the previous years’ scrimmage game.

The event, split between drills with different position groups, non-contact walkthroughs and 11-on-11 live reps, gave fans of the Nittany Lions a closer look at what the team will be like in the upcoming 2026 season.

Here are three takeaways from the first-ever Blue-White Practice.

Rocco Becht’s connection with Koby Howard

Entering the spring practices, Rocco Becht was without his two most familiar targets, Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen, who didn’t participate in 7-on-7 or live reps Saturday, allowing for Koby Howard to continue to grow his connection with his quarterback.

Howard said on Tuesday that they try to get in as many reps as possible when they’re available to help that chemistry grow, and that showed on Saturday.

“I love how we have two weeks until our big May break,” Becht said. “So these next two weeks are gonna be huge in the film room, and obviously if it’s nice out we’ll go outside, rep through some stuff, but really right now until this big May break we’ll be in the film room watching back over spring ball.”

Becht and Howard connected on two passes during their first drive together, including a bullet up the middle for a gain around 15 yards. On the next play, Becht found Howard on a drag route, connecting on consecutive plays.

Campbell emphasized the importance of Becht’s connection and chemistry with the receivers even with him recovering from an injury.

After remaining off the field for a few drives, Becht found Howard right away on the second drive, this time on a corner route that was right in the window for Howard.

Howard emerged as a weapon down the stretch of the 2025 season and was expected to be in contention for the top receiver spot in his sophomore campaign.

Becht and Howard’s chemistry already seems to be strong after just 15 spring practices and could be a key factor in the success of the offense to begin the 2026 season.

James Peoples sees bulk of running back work

With pouring rain throughout the Blue-White Practice, few deep throws were made during the 7-on-7 and 11-on-11 action. Instead, the running backs got the bulk of the work, specifically Ohio State transfer James Peoples.

Despite a three-way battle between Peoples, Carson Hansen and returner Quinton Martin Jr. throughout spring practice, Peoples took a majority of snaps on Saturday.

Hansen started the first few plays in the first 7-on-7 drive before Peoples finished off the drive. When the second team took the field, Peoples was on the field for the first few plays before Martin Jr. made his way on the field.

As Hansen came from Iowa State with Matt Campbell, he's projected to be the starter heading into the regular season. With that in mind, Peoples took the big workload on Saturday as a chance to prove himself and close the gap in the position battle.

When the 11-on-11 action began, Peoples shined with a big cut to the outside, breaking past the first wave of defenders.

However, Hansen made the most of his smaller role in the Blue-White Practice. Hansen had a 10-yard catch after breaking a tackle in 11-on-11, showcasing the team’s potential reliance on Hansen in every facet of the game.

“I don’t know exactly what’s going to happen (with the depth chart),” Hansen said. “We’re just there to compete with each other and make each other better.”

First-team reps didn’t last long as Martin Jr. and other backups replaced a majority of the starters early in the 7-on-7 and 11-on-11.

Cornerbacks shine

The cornerback room features the most returning talent from last season, with Zion Tracy, Daryus Dixson, Audavion Collins and Jahmir Joseph among others coming back to Penn State, and they shined on Saturday.

True freshman Josiah Zayas also put his name on the map during the first few live reps with a pick six after he jumped a screen pass by Peyton Falzone.

“I’ve seen a lot of maturity from (Zayas),” Tracy said. “He was young and came in asking questions that certain freshmen weren't asking. His head was in certain places where you wouldn't think with him being that young.”

Throughout the 7-on-7 drills, Tracy’s aggression was on full display as he was a consistent thorn in Zay Robinson’s side on Rocco Becht’s blind side. Tracy broke up a pass intended for Robinson, giving the wideout no chance on the ball.

Dixson, who rose into a more defined role in the later portion of the 2025 season when A.J. Harris was banged up, made the day’s first interception during the non-contact 11-on-11 drills. He cut off a short pass by Connor Barry and sped down the sideline.

Penn State has only two seniors, including Tracy and Collins, listed on the roster. With such a youthful room, Terry Smith has time to develop for the future while maintaining a strong starting lineup for the 2026 season on the horizon.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State DE Dani Dennis-Sutton drafted by Green Bay Packers with No. 120 pick
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s top edge rusher has made it to the big leagues.

Dani Dennis-Sutton was selected 120th overall by the Green Bay Packers in the fourth round of the 2026 NFL Draft.

The Delaware native returned to Happy Valley for his senior season and was an impact player on the defensive line, earning All-Big Ten third team honors for the second time in his collegiate career. He recorded 42 tackles, 8.5 sacks, and three forced fumbles while leading the FBS with three blocked kicks.

Dennis-Sutton closed his four-year Penn State journey tied for fifth in program history with seven career forced fumbles and sixth with 23.5 sacks. In his final game, his 22 sack yards set the all-time Penn State bowl record to help the Nittany Lions win the 2025 Pinstripe Bowl.

"I feel like I'm respected around here at Penn State,” Dennis-Sutton said at pro day. “I'm never in trouble. I'm never doing dumb stuff, and then I'm always just working. I'm not a guy that's just yelling. I'm not a pep talk type of guy, but I'm the ‘gonna work hard’ type of guy. I'm gonna try and motivate my teammates. I'm gonna try to make sure everybody's held accountable, including myself.”

The Packers were impressed by his air at the NFL Scouting Combine, posting a 39.5 inch vertical jump and 10-foot-11 broad jump. He combined that with speed and agility, running a 4.63-second 40-yard dash and 6.90-second 3-cone drill.

At Penn State Pro Day, Dennis-Sutton benched 26 reps, the highest amongst the Nittany Lions draft class.

“I've been working since I was young to be in this position,” Dennis-Sutton said. “That's why I love working out, because once you start to see results, you start to feel results, things like that. It just motivates me even more to keep going.”

Dennis-Sutton is now the fourth former Nittany Lion to be selected in this year’s draft class and will join star Penn State letterman Micah Parsons on the Packers' defensive line.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State OT Drew Shelton drafted in 4th round by Dallas Cowboys
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has yet another offensive lineman in the NFL.

Drew Shelton was selected 112th overall by the Dallas Cowboys in the fourth round of the 2026 NFL Draft.

“It's been great getting in front of teams,” Shelton said at pro day. “It's a dream come true, like every little kid grows up wanting to be in this position.”

Shelton earned the full-time starting position at left tackle in 2024 and proceeded to become one of Penn State’s top contributors in the trenches. Although he had a rough start to the 2025 season, Shelton heated up in the second half along with the rest of the offensive line.

Shelton didn’t participate in Penn State Pro Day after pulling his hamstring when running his second 40-yard dash at the NFL Scouting Combine. He said he could’ve run through drills at pro day, but knew he wouldn’t be displaying the best version of himself.

Shelton was the second offensive lineman from Penn State to be selected and third Nittany Lion overall.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's basketball earns commitment from Big Ten transfer Petra Bozan
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is up to a full five-player lineup for next year.

The Lady Lions earned a commitment from former Nebraska forward Petra Bozan, marking the fifth transfer this week.

Bozan played 32 games during her sophomore year, making 19 starts. The 6-foot-4 Croatia native averaged 6.6 points and 3.4 rebounds per game.

Penn State now has five players with Bozan, Cristan Carter, Devyn Quigley, Kamrah Banks and Ava Black.

title: Penn State earns commitment from 3-star OT Ryan Robbins
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State went back to the Pittsburgh pipeline for its latest commit.

Offensive tackle Ryan Robbins has committed to Penn State, he announced via Instagram on Saturday ahead of Blue-White Practice.

The 3-star recruit ranks as the No. 723 player, No. 67 offensive tackle and No. 19 player in Pennsylvania. He chose Penn State over Ohio State and Virginia Tech.

Penn State was in on Robbins later in the cycle, giving him an official offer on Feb. 12.

Robbins unofficially visited Penn State on March 31, and has an official visit scheduled for June 19 in Happy Valley.

title: Penn State baseball falls in narrow series opener against Oregon
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Fresh off of two straight wins, Penn State traveled cross-country and began a pivotal conference series against No. 19 Oregon. The Nittany Lions are still searching for their first series win of the campaign and an upset over the Ducks would be huge for the squad going into the last stretch of the regular season.

While Penn State stayed with Oregon throughout the game and possessed the lead a few times, the Ducks scored the go-ahead run in the eighth and won 5-4 over the Nittany Lions.

Though Oregon possesses one of the best pitching staffs in the Big Ten, the blue and white struck first. The Ducks’ ace Will Sanford cruised through the first few innings and held Penn State hitless through two innings. Yet right-hander Colin Fitzgerald also held his own on the mound and shut down Oregon’s explosive offense early.

While both teams were quiet offensively in the beginning, Penn State broke the scoreless tie in the third. Shortstop Preston Yaucher rocketed a leadoff double to left center and the Ducks defense made a costly error on the next play.

With Yaucher in scoring position, left fielder Jayden Davis grounded a routine out to first baseman Brayden Jaksa, but the freshman muffed the throw over. The timely mistake allowed Davis to reach safely, while Yaucher scored from second to give Penn State a 1-0 lead.

Despite cracking the scoring open, little offensive momentum followed as Sanford swiftly struck out the next two Nittany Lions and retired the side in order the following frame. Fitzgerald delivered one of his best starts of the season, but the senior finally surrendered his first hit in the fourth and it caused damage.

Oregon gained some momentum when Angel Laya knocked a one-out single up the middle. A few batters later, Burke-Lee Mabeus smothered a two-run homer over the left center wall and gave the Ducks a 2-1 advantage.

After Oregon finally put together some productive at-bats, Penn State quickly answered back with a run of its own in the fifth. Sanford struck out the first two Nittany Lions, but Davis started a two-out rally with a base knock through the middle.

Penn State’s most reliable bat, designated hitter Michael Anderson, moved Davis into scoring position with a single of his own to left center. Sanford then threw a wild pitch that reached the backstop and Davis scored from third to tie the game up at two.

However, the tie didn’t last long as the Ducks replied in the bottom half of the frame. Jax Gimenez drew a one-out walk and then aggressively stole second with his speed. Jaksa came through with a base knock into center and Ryan Cooney lifted a sacrifice fly to center to reclaim the lead for Oregon, 3-2.

The Nittany Lions left two runners stranded in the sixth, but Fitzgerald kept the deficit at just one run with some clutch pitching later in the frame. Laya led off with a perfect bunt single down the third base line and Maddox Molony followed with a hit of his own to the same side of the diamond.

As pressure mounted, Fitzgerald recorded the crucial first out by striking out Mabeus looking. Yet Naulivou Lauaki Jr. worked a long walk and bases were loaded with just one away. In the biggest position of the game, Fitzgerald kept his composure and struck out Jack Brooks swinging.

Penn State’s ace escaped the inning with no damage after he retired the next Duck off a routine grounder to Yaucher at shortstop. Oregon went to its bullpen at the start of the seventh and brought in right-hander Luke Morgan.

The sophomore quickly retired the blue and white’s first two batters, but lost his control and hit Anderson. The mistake extended the inning and allowed second baseman Bryce Molinaro to come through with a huge swing.

The redshirt junior belted his 12th home run deep to right and put Penn State ahead 4-3 late in the game. With a tight one-run advantage, coach Mike Gambino turned to right-hander Dimond Loosli in the high-leverage situation.

While Loosli has been huge lately in key moments for Penn State, the Ducks jumped on him. Jaksa smacked a leadoff double down the right field line and despite Loosli striking out the next two batters, Laya drew a two-out walk. Molony delivered in the high-pressure situation and knocked an RBI single through the right side to even the game at four.

After Mabeus walked, Gambino pulled Loosli and turned to right-hander Kyle Emmons with runners on the corners. The junior retired the next Duck to end the inning and kept the game tied.

While the Nittany Lions couldn’t take the lead in the top of the eighth, Oregon plated the winning run later in the inning. Brooks pulled a leadoff single to third and then advanced into scoring position off a steal.

Jaksa drew a one-out walk and Emmons quickly faced runners on second and third after joint steals. Drew Smith grounded to Spencer Barnett at third, but he committed an error and Brooks scored.

Penn State couldn’t put together a comeback rally in the ninth and fell in a close contest.

Up Next

Penn State continues its away series in Eugene with the first pitch of game two scheduled at 7:05 p.m. Saturday. The game will be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball opens series on the road against Illinois with shutout win
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State hit the road for its final series in the month of April, taking on Illinois. The series marked a crucial step for the Nittany Lions as they’re still trying to lock in a spot in the 12-team Big Ten Tournament.

As things stood entering Friday’s series opener with the Fighting Illini, the blue and white ranked as the conference’s 10th seed.

Penn State claimed the victory, with a dominant 6-1 win.

Sophomore right-hander Danika Frazier got the nod in the circle for the Fighting Illini, quickly allowing back-to-back singles to shortstop Kaylie Walters and third baseman Allison Oneacre.

An error was committed by Illinois shortstop Ava Moore, but three Nittany Lions on base with no outs. The Moore error came back to bite the Fighting Illini a batter later after Walters reached home on a wild pitch from Frazier.

The blue and white added an extra cushion thanks to an RBI single from first baseman Gabby Gradishar to take a 2-0 lead heading into the home half of the opening inning.

Penn State also turned to a sophomore right-hander to open the series, with Abigail Britton getting the start in the circle.

Britton allowed a walk to third baseman Adisyn Caryl, but the Nittany Lions’ defense otherwise stood tall in its first outing of the night by preventing any other runners from reaching base.

There was no harm done by either side in the second frame. The blue and white went down in order, while Illinois drew another walk against Britton, but couldn’t add anything else to the box score.

In the top of the third inning, a couple of walks and a single presented an opportunity for Penn State to add insurance to their two-run lead. Instead, Frazier escaped the threat, forcing center fielder Natalie Lieto to ground out in a bases-loaded, two-out situation.

Caryl broke through for the Fighting Illini in the bottom of the third with a two-out double in the right-center gap for their first hit of the game, but the threat ended after Eileen Donahue flew out to right field.

The fourth inning was another quiet one as both pitchers remained settled into the circle. Walters was Penn State’s only baserunner after recording an infield single for her second of the game, while Illinois worked two baserunners against Britton due to an error and fielder's choice.

The fifth frame, however, didn’t keep this same energy as the Nittany Lions’ bats came back alive now in their third time around the order.

Designated player Brooke Klosowicz reached on a hard hit ball that was fumbled by Caryl at third base, and this became another costly error for the Fighting Illini. The reason being that left fielder Breanna Hanik launched a first pitch swinging home run in the next at-bat.

The freshman’s eighth long ball of the year put the blue and white up 4-0 heading to the bottom of the fifth.

Junior right-hander McKenna Young entered the circle for Penn State, as Illinois was prepared to bring its top of the order around for the third time.

The Fighting Illini posted their highest threat of the night after putting two runners on via a dropped third strike and a walk. The Nittany Lions answered, though, ending the inning with a double play.

The blue and white came up empty in the top half of the sixth, but Illinois found a breakthrough in its half of the frame.

Donahue, the Fighting Illini’s home run leader, launched one over the center field fence to put the first Illinois run of the night on the board.

First baseman Ellie Haggard built on the momentum with a long single to the right field fence, but the Fighting Illini spark was blown out quickly.

A double play turned by Walters at shortstop for the second consecutive inning silenced the Illinois bats, as Penn State led 4-1 heading into its final at-bat.

The Nittany Lions started its last trip to the plate off strong as Klosowicz led off with a home run, causing the Fighting Illinois to make a change and bring senior right-hander Karley Yergler to the circle.

Yergler allowed Hanik and Gradishar to reach via a walk and fielder's choice attempt, giving the blue and white two on with no outs.

The runners each stole a base to advance to scoring position, and Hanik came around to score on a sacrifice fly from Macy Chamberlin. This was the only other run the blue and white brought across in the inning, leading 6-1 heading into Illinois’ final attempt

Left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris entered for Penn State, to close out Game 1 for the Nittany Lions.

After a couple of fly outs, Taylor Porter singles up the middle in an attempt to start a Fighting Illini rally.

Kellepouris shut down Illinois’ hopes, recording a strikeout to complete the blue and white’s all-around victory.

Up Next

Game 2 between Penn State and Illinois is set for 2 p.m. Saturday.

title: Former Penn State QB Drew Allar drafted 76th overall by Pittsburgh Steelers
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A Penn State quarterback has been drafted in the first three rounds for the first time since 2016.

Drew Allar was selected in the third round by the Pittsburgh Steelers with the 76th overall pick in the 2026 NFL Draft, making him the fourth quarterback off the board. He's now the fifth former Nittany Lion on the Steelers' roster.

Entering the draft, Allar was expected to be a Day 2 pick as one of the more polarizing quarterbacks available, and that’s exactly where he ended up. Allar could possibly sit behind Aaron Rodgers his rookie season, barring Rodgers returns, or he'll be competing with Will Howard for the starting job.

Allar finished his collegiate career with 7,402 passing yards, 61 touchdowns, 13 interceptions and a 63.2% completion percentage, which ranks first in program history. He led Penn State to its first College Football Playoff appearance in 2024, taking the Nittany Lions all the way to the semifinals.

He could’ve taken them even further if it wasn’t for his infamous interception late against Notre Dame while attempting to lead a game-winning drive. It’s plays like those that left many hesitant about Allar’s ability to perform on the big stages.

Allar had plenty of other struggles against elite opponents, such as his game-losing interception against Oregon in 2025 and his poor performances against Ohio State and Michigan.

Regardless, Allar still had one of the best arms available in the draft, if not the best, which likely led to the Steelers taking a chance on the project.

“I think I've shown them throughout my junior year and then this past year, and then leading it through the combine, like the growth and maturity I've made,” Allar said at pro day. “Honestly, it was more of teams asking me what I thought my weaknesses were, not really the teams coming out and saying that. Because then I would, I would ask them, like, ‘what do you guys think?’ They pretty much would agree with me on the things I was saying.”

Allar was highly regarded as one of the best quarterbacks in the nation ahead of the 2025 season, but he struggled to start the year before suffering a broken ankle in the waning moments of Penn State’s loss to Northwestern that ultimately led to James Franklin’s firing.

The Ohio native later called that weekend the worst of his life, but he said at pro day the injury allowed him to reset and focus on himself.

“That was the first injury I really ever had. I had a couple of knicks and bruises throughout my career, but everybody does, but that was my first surgery and first extensive rehab process,” Allar said. “It's definitely a different grind. It just kind of reaffirmed everything that I thought beforehand, and then also gave me a good mental and physical reset to kind of lock back into myself and focus on myself and getting myself better.”

Allar will now look to be the first Penn State quarterback to carve out a career as an NFL starter since Kerry Collins, who played in the league for 17 seasons.

title: Penn State women's basketball earns commitment from Georgetown's Cristen Carter
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
The first forward on Tanisha Wright's squad is confirmed.

Former Georgetown forward Cristen Carter announced her commitment to Penn State on Friday, marking the fourth player on next year's team.

Carter spent the 2025-26 season with the Hoyas, where she made five starts, averaging 4.6 points and 4.1 rebounds per game. The Indianapolis native joins assistant coach Kelly Mazzante with the Lady Lions after they both were on Georgetown last year.

Before her time with the Hoyas, Carter spent two years at Butler. There, she averaged a career-high 5.3 points per game her sophomore year, making a combined 30 starts between the two seasons.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's basketball transfer Justin Houser signs with Florida Gulf Coast
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Former Penn State 7-footer Justin Houser will continue his career in the A-Sun conference.

Houser has signed with Florida Gulf Coast, the team announced Friday. The Eagles are led by ex-Penn State head coach Pat Chambers.

𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐞𝐝 ✍️welcome to FGCU, Justin!#ATTITUDE // #WingsUp pic.twitter.com/sqrVsVwTOA— FGCU Men's Hoops (@FGCU_MBB) April 24, 2026

The Nittany Lions originally planned for Houser to potentially redshirt this past season as a depth piece behind 7-footer Yanic Konan Niederhauser. However, with Konan Niederhauser leaving the team for the 2025 NBA draft, Houser was thrust into action in 2025-26.

Houser saw increased minutes in early February as Penn State was navigating injury issues in the frontcourt. He ended up averaging 1.4 points and 1.1 rebounds per game with 42.9% shooting from the field across 18 appearances.

The Camp Hill, Pennsylvania, native led the Nittany Lions in blocks this past season, registering eight of them. He had three blocks in his performance against USC in February.

Houser has three years of eligibility remaining as he joins Florida Gulf Coast.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State women's basketball guard Shaelyn Steele transfers to Tennessee
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After two years donning the blue and white, Shaelyn Steele is off to new things.

Steele committed to Tennessee on Friday, marking the ninth player to find her new home.

The Ashland, Kentucky, native was the last of Penn State's 10 returning players to enter the transfer portal, leaving on April 7.

In her final season with the blue and white, Steele averaged just 1.8 points and 2.7 rebounds per game in eight starts.

Like Penn State, the Lady Vols lost all of its eligible returning players to the transfer portal during the offseason as coach Kim Caldwell has worked to rebuild her roster. With Steele, Tennessee now has seven players.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball to play Princeton in EIVA Championship
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following a big win against Charleston, Penn State is gearing up for the EIVA Championship against Princeton.

As the No. 1 seed, the blue and white were guaranteed hosting rights for the tournament and a first-round bye, dropping them in at the EIVA Semifinal. In a must-win scenario, it pulled out a critical 3-2 win.

The Nittany Lions have gone five sets in the last five matches played, but it doesn’t seem to be wearing on their stamina.

In fact, Penn State had three players hit double digits in kills on Thursday. Owen Rose, Sean Harvey and Tristan Hassell all played huge games, totaling a combined 45 kills against the Golden Eagles.

Rose has been a key piece to the success of the blue and white this season. He had a season-high 10 kills against Charleston, while also tallying five blocks and one ace.

Harvey continues to be a threat to any defense that plays opposite of the blue and white. As EIVA Player of the Year, the redshirt sophomore has hit double digits in kills in his last seven matches, totaling 19 on Thursday in the semifinal.

While Hassell is just a freshman, he’s shown great promise over the last few games.

In the last regular-season match of the year, the opposite hitter had a season-high 26 kills against George Mason. He totaled seven digs that same game, setting another season-high.

It was no different in the semifinal match. Hassell had 16 kills and hit .448 against the Charleston squad.

Despite being on a 10-game winning streak and still holding an undefeated record in the EIVA, it won’t be a walk in the park for the Nittany Lions on Saturday.

No. 6-seed Princeton went the distance against NJIT in the semifinal match on Thursday. After falling 37-35 in a first set that took nearly an hour to complete, the Tigers came back and took the win in five sets.

Owen Mellon had a strong performance in Thursday’s match. With 20 kills and seven digs, the senior was a key part of the thrilling win for the Tigers.

Ambrose Engling was another highlight for the Princeton squad against the Highlanders. The sophomore came second in kills with 19, marking a season-high.

Engling also had a season-high 13 digs and five blocks in the same match.

If there’s any room to get easy points, it might come in the serving game for both sides.

The serve game has been a rough spot for the blue and white all year. With eight service aces to 16 service errors Thursday, it needs to eliminate unnecessary errors to prevent easy scoring.

The same can be said about the Tigers. With three aces to 13 errors against NJIT, it’s imperative that these errors are worked out before the championship match.

With two teams full of standout players, the match is sure to be well-fought. And if the five-set outcome in both semifinal matches is a reflection of the way both teams play, it’s likely to be a battle on Saturday.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's basketball adds Danielle Edwards as assistant coach, director of recruiting
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coach Tanisha Wright's squad has added another member to its coaching staff.

Former Rutgers assistant coach Danielle Edwards was hired as Penn State's newest assistant coach and director of recruiting on Friday.

Welcome to Happy Valley, @edwards__dani! 🦁#WeAre pic.twitter.com/EI45Qw5siu— Penn State Women's Basketball (@PennStateWBB) April 24, 2026

Edwards spent last season with the Scarlet Knights as an assistant coach and director of recruiting operations.

Prior to her time in Piscataway, New Jersey, she spent four years at SMU. Edwards' time there began as director of player personnel before becoming an assistant coach and recruiting coordinator.

During the 2022-23 season, Edwards helped the Mustangs to her best season with a 17-13 record.

Before her time with SMU, Edwards was the director of player personnel at Michigan for two seasons for her first coaching opportunity.

Edwards graduated from Clemson in 2019 following one of the best playing careers in Tigers' history. The Perry Hall, Maryland, native made 123 starts, the most in program history, finished top-five career steals with 291 steals and finished 14th in all-time scoring with 1,274 career points.

Edwards was a two-time All-ACC Defensive Team selection and helped her team to an NCAA Tournament second-round appearance in her senior year.
